question_text: In which of the following places is the Tomb of Ahmad Shah Al Wali Bahmani built? [SSC CGL 30/06/2023]
solution:
(C) Ashtoor
Ashtoor (Ashtur) is known as necropolis (cemetery) of Bahmani Sultans. It is located near Bidar in Karnataka.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Bahmani Monuments at Gulbarga, Karnataka represent ________ style of architecture. [SSC MTS 03/05/2023]
solution:
(C) Deccan
It is the mix of Persian and local Indian architecture. Examples - Gulbarga Fort, Haft Gumbaz, Madrasa of Mahmud Gawan, Bidar Fort, Gol Gumbaz (Bijapur), Jama Masjid (Gulbarga), Bahmani Tombs at Ashtur etc.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The craftspersons of Bidar were so famed for their inlay work in ________ [SSC CGL 08/12/2022]
solution:
(D) Copper and Silver
The crafts persons of Bidar were famed for their inlay work of copper and silver, it came to be called 'Bidri'.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Which of the following statements about Amaranayakas of Vijayanagara are correct? [CAPF 2021]
1. The Amaranayakas were military commanders who were given territories to govern by the Raya.
2. In lieu of their service and also to maintain their contingents, they were paid in cash.
3. They maintained stipulated contingents and elephants.
4. They used to send tribute to the king annually and personally appeared in the royal court with gifts to express their loyalty.
solution:
(B) 1, 3 and 4 only
The Amara-Nayakas were military commanders, who were granted territories (Amaram) with a fixed revenue in lieu of their salaries and not paid in cash. The Amara-Nayakas were granted territories to govern, collect taxes, and maintain a contingent of troops and elephants, in return for which they provided military service and tribute to the king.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Battle of Rakshasi-Tangadi is popularly known as the ________ [CAPF 2022]
solution:
(A) Battle of Talikota
[ref Q-16 level 1]

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Abdur Razzak, the traveler to Vijayanagar, had referred to the presence of a number of flower merchants in Vijayanagar and noted a particular flower that seemed as necessary as food to the people of that part. Which variety of flower was being referred to by Abdur Razzak? [CAPF 2023]
solution:
(A) Jasmine
Abdur Razzaq was a Persian Scholar and ambassador of Shah Rukh, a ruler of Timurid dynasty, who visited Vijayanagar Empire during the reign of Devaraya II in 1440s. He wrote that people of Vijayanagar considered Jasmine as essential as food.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Domingo Paes describes a king in these words: 'Of medium height, and of fair complexion and good figure, rather fat than thin; he has on his face signs of smallpox'. Identify the king from the options given below. [NDA II 2024]
solution:
(A) Krishnadeva Raya
[ref Q-5 level 2]

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Domingo Paes, the medieval traveller, has described the city of ________. [CDS II 2021]
solution:
(B) Vijayanagara
Domingo Paes was a Portuguese traveller, visited Vijayanagar Empire around 1520 during the reign of King Krishna Deva Raya. He compared medieval Vijayanagar to Rome in his work 'Chronica dos Reis de Bisnaga' (Chronicle of the Vijayanagar Kings).

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which one of the following works was composed by Krishnadevaraya on statecraft in Telugu? [CDS I 2022]
solution:
(A) Amuktamalyada
Amuktamalyada was composed by Krishna Deva Raya in Telugu language about polity and statecraft. Jambavati Kalyanam was a Sanskrit drama by Krishna Deva Raya, depicting the marriage of Jambavati, daughter of Jambavan (bear king) to Lord Krishna. Rayavachakamu, a Telugu prose work written by Vishvanatha Nayani, a governor in the court of Krishna Deva Raya. Satyavadu Parinaya was written by Krishna Deva Raya in Sanskrit. Other Sanskrit works - Madalasa Charita and Rasamanjari.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which two rivers flow in the region between Gulbarga and Vijayanagara Hampi? [CDS I 2023]
solution:
(D) Krishna and Tungabhadra
Krishna river flows in the southern side of Gulbarga district. Hampi is situated on the banks of Tungabhadra river, which is a tributary of Krishna river and formed by the confluence of Tunga and Bhadra rivers.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Abdur Razzaq, an ambassador of the ruler of Persia, was sent to Calicut in the fifteenth century. He was greatly impressed by the: [CDS II 2023]

options.b: Climatic conditions of the coastal town
options.c: High quality spices of the place

solution:
(A) Fortifications of Calicut
Abdur Razzaq wrote 'Matla-us-Sadain wa Majma-ul-Bahrain' (The Rising Place of Two Lights and the Confluence of Two Oceans) about his visit. He praised City's fortifications, which included seven levels of forts, constructed without mortar or cementing agent. 1-4 levels - to protect agricultural fields and granaries. 5-7 levels - consists Royal and Sacred Centres, markets etc.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Vijayanagara rulers claimed that they were ruling on behalf of: [NDA II 2024]
solution:
(C) Shri Virupaksha
Shri Virupaksha, a form of Shiva, was the tutelary deity of the Vijayanagar kings and state. The rulers claimed to rule on behalf of Shri Virupaksha. The royal orders during Vijayanagar period were signed with the name of the deity.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was not a court poet of king Krishnadevaraya? [CDS I 2024]
solution:
(D) Siddheshwara
Ashtadiggajas of Krishnadevaraya - Allasani Peddana, Nandi Timmana, Madayyagari Mallana, Dhurjati, Ayyalaraju Ramabhadrudu, Pingali Surana, Ramarajabhushanudu, and Tenali Ramakrishna. Tenali Rama wrote Panduranga Mahatyam, it is considered as one of the five great Telugu Kavyas.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Consider the following pairs: [CDS II 2024]
1. Gahapati - Name of a powerful Dynasty in Orissa
2. Ashvapati - Deccan Sultans
3. Narapati - Rayas of Vijayanagara
Which of the above pairs is/are correctly matched?
solution:
(C) 1, 2 and 3
Gajapati - powerful dynasty in Orissa, founder - Kapilendra Deva of Suryavamsa lineage. Ashvapati - Deccan Sultans. It means 'Lord of Horses'. Narapati - Rayas of Vijayanagar. It means 'Lord of Men'.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Krishnadevaraya's capital city Vijayanagara was situated on the banks of [CISF AC 2021]
solution:
(A) Tungabhadra
Vijayanagar is located in Karnataka, along the banks of Tungabhadra river. Vijayanagar was a well-planned fortified city with Indo-Islamic architectural styles. Virupaksha Temple, Vitthal Swami temple, Kamalapuram Tank, Hazara Rama temple, Elephant stable, Lotus Palace etc. were found in Vijayanagar.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following rulers of Vijayanagara Empire constructed a large dam across Tungabhadra River and a canal-cum-aqueduct several kilometres long from the river to the capital city? [IAS 2023]
solution:
(A) Devaraya I
Devaraya I (1406-22 AD) He was the third son of Harihara II. His greatest achievements was the irrigation works where a dam was built across Tungabhadra, with canals leading to city. Nicolo de Conti, Italian traveller visited during his reign.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who of the following rulers of medieval India gave permission to the Portuguese to build a fort at Bhatkal? [IAS 2024]
solution:
(A) Krishnadevaraya
Alphonsa de Albuquerque, Portuguese governor who built a fort at Bhatkal in Karnataka in 1510 with the permission of Vijayanagar ruler Krishna Deva Raya.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The structure of Mahanavami Dibba is situated in which one of the following places? [GEOSCIENTIST 2023]
solution:
(C) Hampi
Mahanavami Dibba was a large, elevated stone platform located in the royal enclosure of Hampi. It was the royal seat during Vijayanagar Empire's Navratri celebrations. It was featuring relief carvings and also known as Dasara Dibba. It was spread across 11,000 sq ft and 40 ft in height.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Consider the following statements about the 'Mahanavami Dibba': [NDA I 2021]
1. It was the name of a giant box of sweets distributed at the Mahanavami festival.
2. It was the name of a massive platform with a base covered with relief carvings.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
solution:
(B) 2 only
Mahanavami Dibba was a massive platform with base covered in relief carvings, not a giant box of sweets. It was a significant structure used by the Vijayanagar Kings for royal ceremonies during the Mahanavami festival.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Which of the following statements about Vijayanagara king Krishnadevaraya's expeditions are correct? [NDA I 2024]
1. In the early years of his reign he marched against the ruler of Orissa.
2. In 1520 CE, he captured the fort of Raichur from Ismail Adil Khan.
3. Krishnadevaraya restored Sultan Mahmud Shah to power and assumed the title of Yavana-rajya-sthapan-acharya.
Select the answer using the code given below:
solution:
(C) 1, 2 and 3
Krishnadevaraya marched against the ruler of Orissa, Gajapati Prataparudra, to expand his territory. The battle of Raichur in 1520 CE was fought between Vijayanagar Empire and the Sultanate of Bijapur. Krishnadevaraya successfully captured the Raichur fort from Ismail Adil Shah, ruler of Bijapur. Krishnadevaraya restored Sultan Mahmud Shah to the throne of Bidar and assumed the title of 'Yavana-rajya-sthapan-acharya', means 'Restorer of the Yavana Kingdom'.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Consider the following statements regarding the royal center in Vijayanagara Empire: [NDA II 2023]
1. The royal center was located in the south-western part of the settlement.
2. It included over 60 temples.
3. About 30 building complexes at the site have been identified as palaces.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
solution:
(C) 1, 2 and 3
The Royal Centre of Vijayanagar was the seat of power and administration. It was located in the southwestern part of the settlement. It was a major religious centre, with over 60 temples of various Hindu deities and about 30 building complexes, identified as palaces.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was given the charge of looking after the imperial household during the Mughal administration? [SSC CHSL 17/08/2023]
solution:
(A) Mir Saman
Mir Saman also known as Khan Saman, was in charge of Imperial household. Mir Bahr - officer in charge of Naval affairs related to seaports, riverports. Mir Bakshi - head of military department. Mir-i-Atish (Daroga-i-Topkhana) - head of the artillery department.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Name the French jeweller who traveled to India at least six times during the Mughal period [SSC CGL 17/07/2023]
solution:
(B) Jean-Baptiste Tavernier
Jean-Baptiste Tavernier (French) - six trips to India between 1630 to 1668, during reign of Shah Jahan and Aurangzeb. He wrote a book 'Travels in India'. He collected precious stones and gems for his business in Europe. Antonio Monserrate (Spanish Jesuit monk) came to India in 1578, during the rule of Akbar. Seydi Ali Reis, Ottoman admiral, came to India in 1554. Peter Mundy (English) came to India in 1628.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who built the Pathar Ki Masjid in Patna, Bihar? [SSC CHSL 14/03/2023]
solution:
(B) Parwez Shah
Pathar Ki Masjid, also known as Saif Khan's Mosque, in Patna (Bihar). Built by Parwez Shah, son of Jahangir in 1621. Feroz Shah Tughlaq built Feroz Shah Kotla Fort in 1354. Aurangzeb built Badshahi Mosque (Lahore), Bibi ka Maqbara. Alauddin Khilji built Alai Darwaza, Siri Fort, Hazar Sutun.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The real name of Babur, the founder of Mughal dynasty in India was: [SSC CGL 24/09/2024]
solution:
(A) Zahiruddin Muhammad
Babur (1526-30 AD) established Mughal dynasty. Original name - Zahiruddin Muhammad. Called themselves as 'Uzbeks'. Babur ascended the throne of Ferghana at the age of 12 years in 1494.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Mughal emperor, Babur was a devotee of the ________ Sufi Silsila [SSC MTS 01/10/2024]
solution:
(C) Naqshbandi
Babur was a follower of Naqshbandi Sufi order and was a devotee of Naqshbandi leader Khwaja Ubaidullah Ahrar. This order was founded by Bahibillah. Suhrawardi order was established in India by Bahauddin Zakanya. Chisti order was established by Khwaja Moinuddin Chisti in India. Qadri order was established in India by Niyammad-ulla-Qadiri.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following Mughal Emperors had assumed the title of Padshah? [SSC MTS 12/05/2023]
solution:
(B) Babur
Babur declared himself as 'Padshah' or 'Badshah' which means emperor. He also held the title of 'Ghazi'. Babur was a Timurid Prince, the descendant of both Timur (father's side) and Ghenghis Khan (mother's side).

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The First Battle of Panipat (1526) marked the beginning of: [SSC MTS 14/11/2024]

options.c: British East India company's rule in India
options.d: French East India Company's rule in India

solution:
(B) Mughal's rule in India
First Battle of Panipat (1526): fought between Babur and Sultan of Delhi Ibrahim Lodi on 21 April, 1526, in Panipat (Haryana). Babur emerged victorious and established Mughal Empire in India. Babur used Tulughma and Araba war tactics. Babur was invited by Daulat Khan Lodi, Punjab governor under Ibrahim Lodi.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The last Mughal emperor of India was: [SSC MTS 12/11/2024]
solution:
(C) Bahadur Shah II
[ref Q-107]

subject: Medieval History
question_text: After years of wandering Babur seized Kabul in ________. [SSC MTS 19/05/2023]
solution:
(B) 1504
Babur besieged Kabul in 1504 from the Arghuns under Mukim Beg Arghun and became the ruler of Kabul and Ghazni regions.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following battles was fought between Babur and Rana Sanga in 1527? [SSC CGL 11/09/2024]
solution:
(C) Battle of Khanwa
Battle of Khanwa was fought in March 1527. Fought between Kingdom of Mewar, led by Rana Sanga and the Mughal Empire, led by Babur. Babur emerged victorious. Hasan Khan of Mewat, Mahmud Lodi and other Hindu Rajput kings joined Rana Sanga in this battle. Babur defeated Medini Rai of Chanderi in Battle of Chanderi (1528).

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who was the Mughal Emperor at the time of Battle of Plassey? [SSC CHSL 26/10/2020]
solution:
(A) Alamgir II
Battle of Plassey (1757) was fought between British East India Company, led by Robert Clive against the Nawab of Bengal Siraj-ud-Daulah. Clive bribed Mir Jafar, commander of Nawab's army. Siraj-ud-Daulah was defeated. Mughal Emperor during Battle of Plassey was Alamgir II (1754 to 1759). Original name - Mirza Aziz-ud-Din Muhammad.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: 'Kabuli Bagh Mosque' of Panipat was constructed by which Mughal ruler? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 11/12/2024]
solution:
(D) Babur
Kabuli Bagh Mosque in Panipat was built by Babur in 1527, to mark his victory over Ibrahim Lodi in first Battle of Panipat in 1526. The mosque is named after Kabuli Begum, wife of Babur.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was forced to leave his ancestral throne due to the invasion of the Uzbeks? [SSC JE 05/06/2024]
solution:
(B) Babur
Babur was forced to leave his ancestral throne in Ferghana when Uzbeks (another Mongol group) under the leadership of Shaibani Khan attacked in late 15th century.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following is the meaning of "Pistaq" in the context of medieval Indo-Islamic Architecture? [SSC CGL 16/08/2021]
solution:
(B) Tall gateway
In Indo-Islamic architecture, 'Pistaq' refers to a high, centrally situated entrance or tall gateway, decorated with calligraphy, tilework and geometric designs. Dome was called as 'Gumbad' of 'Qubba'. Water tanks were called as Hauz or Baoli.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: During the Mughal period, the term "milkiyat" denoted which of the following? [SSC CGL 16/08/2021]
solution:
(C) Personal land of Zamindars
Milkiyat refers to the extensive personal lands held by zamindars (landowners), which they could cultivate, sell or mortgage as they wished. The term 'milk' or 'Madad-i-Mash' in Mughal and 'shasan' in Rajasthan, referred to revenue free land grants.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In his autobiography, Babur described his interest in planning and laying out formal gardens, placed within rectangular walled enclosures and divided into four quarters by artificial channels. These gardens are known as ________ [SSC MTS 05/11/2024]
solution:
(A) Char Bagh
Char Bagh style garden was originated in Persia, introduced in India by Babur. The garden is divided into 4 parts by pathways or waterways. Gardens at Taj Mahal, Humayun's Tomb, Shalimar Gardens (Lahore) are examples of Char Bagh style gardens.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who made the use of Gunpowder common in North India to defeat enemies? [SSC CPO 03/10/2023]
solution:
(B) Babur
Babur was the first to use the gunpowder in India during the first Battle of Panipat in 1526 against Ibrahim Lodi. Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi and established Mughal Empire. He ruled from 1526 - 1530.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who was the eldest of four sons of Babur? [SSC CPO 04/10/2023]
solution:
(B) Humayun
Babur had four sons - Humayun (eldest), Kamran, Hindal and Askari. Humayun inherited the Mughal Empire and ruled it during 1530-1540 and 1555 - 1556. Kamran ruled in Kabul and Kandahar. Hindal and Askari received Sambhal and Alwar.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Babur defeated Mahmud Lodi and Sultan Nusrat Shah thus establishing Mughal rule in India in the Battle of ________ in 1529. [SSC MTS 04/05/2023]
solution:
(C) Ghagra
Battle of Ghaghra (1529): fought between Babur against the coalition of Afghan forces including Mahmud Lodi (brother of Ibrahim Lodi) and Sultan Nusrat Shah, Sultan of Bengal. Babur won this battle and expanded the Mughal empire in East.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: 'Memoirs of Babur' or 'Baburnama', also known as 'Tuzk-e Babri', was written by: [SSC CHSL 06/08/2021]
solution:
(C) Babur
Tuzuk-i-Baburi, the autobiography of Babur originally written in Turkish language, was translated in Persian as 'Baburnama' by Abdul Rahim Khan-i-Khana and later into English by Madam Annette Beveridge. Babur also compiled two anthologies of poems, Diwan (in Turkish) and Mubaiyan (in Persian). He also wrote Risal-i-Usaz or letters of Babur.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The famous monument, Aram Bagh, was built by Babur. It is located in ________. [SSC CHSL 09/03/2023]
solution:
(B) Uttar Pradesh
Aram Bagh (also known as Bagh-i-Gulafshan) was built by Babur in Agra, UP. It is the oldest Mughal Garden in India. Babur also built Kabuli Masjid in Panipat and Jama Masjid in Sambhal.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Where was Babur buried for the first time in 1530? [SSC PHASE X 02/08/2022]
solution:
(B) Agra
Babur was buried first time in 1530 in Aram Bagh (Agra), and later his remains were taken to Kabul as per Babur's wish and reburied in the Bagh-e-Babur garden in Kabul by his Afghan wife Bibi Mubarika.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was the successor of Mughal Emperor, Babur? [SSC CGL 17/09/2024]
solution:
(C) Humayun
Humayun (1530-40 and 1555-56 AD), eldest son of Babur. Ascended the throne in 1530. Also known as Mirza Nasir-ud-Din Muhammad. Fought Battle of Chausa (1539) and Battle of Kannauj (1540) against Sher Shah Suri (Afghan) and lost both battles. He escaped to Iran and took shelter at Safavid dynasty. He returned in 1555 and defeated his brothers and the Afghans. His sister Gulbadan Begum wrote his biography 'Humayunama'.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: As per the Timurid tradition, Humayan had to share power with his brothers. Humayun had the control of Delhi, Agra and Central India, while his brother Kamran controlled which of the following regions? [SSC CGL 19/09/2024]
solution:
(C) Afghanistan and Punjab
Kamran (Humayun's brother) ruled Kabul and Kandahar region of Afghanistan and parts of Punjab. Humayun defeated Bahadur Shah of Gujarat and Malwa and gave his territories to Askari, but Askari lost both the territories to Bahadur Shah.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The construction of Humayun's tomb was completed by: [SSC MTS 28/10/2024 + SSC CHSL 13/03/2023]
solution:
(B) Biga Begum
Humayun's tomb was built by his wife Bega Begum (Haji Begum) in Delhi. It was the first garden tomb in Mughal art and architecture. It was a UNESCO site (1993).

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following is an example of the first garden-tomb on the Indian subcontinent? [SSC MTS 22/10/2024]
solution:
(D) Humayun's Tomb
Humayun died in 1556, in his library Sher Mandal in Delhi. His tomb was the first garden tomb of Indian subcontinent.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following cities was built by Humayun in Delhi? [SSC CHSL 03/08/2023]
solution:
(B) Dinpanah
Humayun built the city of Din Panah (means 'Refuge of Religion') in 1533, on the banks of Yamuna River. Sher Shah Suri destroyed Din Panah and built his new capital, the Sher Shahi, now known as Purana Qila fort.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Ahmadnagar was finally annexed by the Mughals in ________. [SSC CGL 06/12/2022]
solution:
(B) 1637
Ahmednagar was ruled by Nizam Shahi dynasty from 1490-1637. Mughals waged wars against Ahmadnagar from Akbar's reign but completely annexed Ahmadnagar during the rule of Shah Jahan in 1637. This annexation marked the end of Nizam Shahi dynasty.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: After the death of Humayun, the 13-year-old Akbar was coronated in 1556 at Kalanaur in ________. [SSC CGL 10/09/2024]
solution:
(C) Punjab
Humayun died due to a fall from staircase of his library in 1556 at Din Panah and was buried in Delhi. After his death, 13 years old Akbar was coronated in 1556 at Kalanaur in Punjab. The place where Akbar was crowned is known as Takht-i-Akbari.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In 1539, the Battle of Chausa was fought between Humayun and ________. [SSC CGL 19/09/2024]
solution:
(B) Sher Shah Suri
Battle of Chausa (1539): fought between Humayun and Sher Shah Suri (original name - Farid) an Afghan. Humayun was assisted by Gajpati Ujjainia, Ujjainiya Rajput ruler. Farid Khan defeated Humayun and took the title of 'Sher Shah'. Humayun escaped from the battlefield to save his life.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Battle of Kannauj was fought between Sher Shah Suri and the Mughals led by ________. [SSC CPO 28/06/2024]
solution:
(A) Humayun
Battle of Kannauj or Bilgram (1540): fought between Sher Shah and Humayun. Sher Shah defeated Humayun and He escaped from India and spent 15 years in exile. He returned after the death of Sher Shah and regained his empire in 1555 AD.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: At which of the following places is the tomb of Sher Shah Suri situated? [SSC MTS 04/11/2024 + SSC CHSL 02/08/2023]
solution:
(B) Sasaram
Sher Shah Suri - original name - Farid Khan. He died in a gunpowder explosion in Kalinjar in 1545 AD and was buried in Sasaram, Bihar. Sher Shah was succeeded by Islam Shah (1545-54) and Islam Shah was succeeded by Muhammad Adil Shah (1554-55). Sher Shah conquested: Malwa (1542), Ranthambore (1542), Raisin (1543), Rajputana-annexation of Marwar (1542), Chittor (1544) and Kalinjar (1545).

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following medieval rulers focused his attention on the construction of sarais (shelter) for the convenience of travellers? [SSC CHSL 10/08/2023]
solution:
(B) Sher Shah Suri
Sher Shah Suri (1540-45): original name - Farid Khan. He defeated Humayun in Battle of Chausa (1539) and Battle of Kannauj (1540). He issued coin called Rupia and fixed standard weights and measures all over empire. Built Sarais (shelter houses) for convenience of travellers through G.T. Road. Land Revenue System: 1/3rd of the land was fixed as land tax. The peasants were given Patta and Qabuliyat which fixed the peasant's rights and taxes. He removed Zamindars and collected taxes directly.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following rulers is credited with the construction of Purana Quila in Delhi? [SSC CHSL 28/06/2023]
solution:
(D) Humayun and Sher Shah
Humayun started the construction of Purana Qila, in Dinpanah, city constructed by him in 1533. It was destroyed by Sher Shah in 1540 and he reconstructed the Purana Qila and completed fortifications of the city.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The historical Sher-Shah Suri Marg runs between ________. [SSC MTS 14/06/2023]
solution:
(C) Delhi and Amritsar
The historical Sher Shah Suri Marg, also known as National Highway 1, runs between Delhi and Amritsar. It is a part of the broader Grand Trunk Road (G.T. Road) constructed by Sher Shah from Chittagong in Bangladesh to Kabul in Afghanistan. This road is also known as Sadak-e-Azam road.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was one of the Navratnas of Akbar? [SSC CGL 11/09/2024]
solution:
(C) Tansen
Navratnas of Akbar: Birbal (administrator), Abul Fazl (scholar and statesman), Faizi (scholar), Todar Mal (finance minister), Bhagwandas, Man Singh, Tansen (Musician), Abdul Rahim Khan-i-Khana (statesman, hindi poet), Mulla Do Piyaza.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who was the revenue minister during the reign of Akbar? [SSC CGL 09/09/2024]
solution:
(C) Todar Mal
Raja Todar Mal was the finance (revenue) minister and among the Navratnas of Akbar. He introduced Zabt and Dahshala system of taxation. Mulla-Do-Piyaza was the prominent advisor and minister in Akbar's reign.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Tansen, the music legend, was a courtier in which of the following courts before coming to Akbar? [SSC CPO 04/10/2023]
solution:
(A) Rewa
Tansen served under Raja Ramchandra Singh of Tomar dynasty of Gwalior. He belonged to Gwalior/Rewa Gharana. Original name - Ramtanu Pande. He composed Dhrupad raag and played Rudraveena (Rebab). Title 'Mian' was given to Tansen by Akbar. Swami Haridas was the guru of Tansen.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was the Vakil under the reign of Mughal Emperor, Akbar, who received the title of Khan-i-Khanan? [SSC CGL 13/09/2024]
solution:
(B) Bairam Khan
Bairam Khan was military commander and regent of Mughal Empire. He was the mentor and guardian of Akbar after Humayun's death. Led the Mughal side in Second Battle of Panipat. He was given title of 'Khan-i-Khanan' by Humayun. Bairam Khan was murdered by Afghan soldiers while travelling to Mecca.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which amongst the following statements is correct about Mughal emperor Akbar? [SSC CHSL 08/07/2024]

options.a: He fought and won the third battle of Panipat against the Hindu King Hemu.
options.b: He founded a new religion called 'Din-e Ilahi'.
options.d: He was highly educated and knew many languages.

solution:
(B) He founded a new religion called 'Din-e Ilahi'.
Akbar founded a new religion 'Din-e-Ilahi' (Divine faith or Religion of God) in 1581. It was based on the values of Hinduism, Islam, Janism, Christianity. It followed the concept of Single God. Birbal was the only Hindu who followed this religion. Akbar fought Second Battle of Panipat in 1556 against Hindu king Hemu. Akbar was declared emperor in 1556. He was known to be illiterate, and can only communicate in languages like Persian, Arabic and Hindi.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who amongst the following Mughal emperors got classical Hindu mythological texts Ramayana and Mahabharata translated into Persian? [SSC CHSL 09/07/2024]
solution:
(A) Akbar
Akbar established a 'Maktab Khana' (translation department) for translation of Sanskrit texts into Persian. Ramayana was translated into Persian by Abdul Qadir Badayuni. Mahabharata was translated by Faizi and Abdul Qadir and was called 'Razmnama'.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: To which position in the Mughal administration under Akbar does Diwan-i-Ala refer? [SSC MTS 04/11/2024]
solution:
(B) Finance Minister
Diwan-i-Ala refers to revenue of finance minister. Other officers - Subedar (Governor), Bakshis (military paymasters), Sadr (religious department), Kotwals (Police).

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Akbar's first expedition towards Deccan was sent to Ahmednagar under the leadership of Prince Murad and ________. [SSC MTS 04/11/2024]
solution:
(C) Abdul Rahim Khan-i-Khanan
In 1591, Akbar adapted a policy of aggression towards the Deccan and sent an expedition to the Deccan under the command of Prince Murad (governor of Gujarat) and Abdul Rahim Khan-I-Khanan.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Amer fort was built with red sandstone and white marble by which ruler in the 16 century? [SSC MTS 09/10/2024]
solution:
(B) Raja Man Singh I
Amer Fort was commissioned by Raja Man Singh I in 1592. He was among the navratnas of Akbar. Man Singh was the Mansabdar of 7000, highest in Akbar's court except his son Jahangir. Amer Fort along with Chittor Fort, Kumbhalgarh Fort, Ranthambore Fort, Gagron Fort and Jaisalmer fort was declared a UNESCO site as 'Hill forts of Rajasthan' group in 2013.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following rulers introduced the 'jharokha darshan' in the Mughal dynasty? [SSC MTS 09/10/2024]
solution:
(D) Akbar
Akbar (1556-1605 AD): full name - Abu'l-Fatah Jalal-ud-din Muhammad Akbar. Eldest son of Humayun. Ascended the throne at the age of 13 in Kalanaur, Punjab. Akbar married to Harka Bai, daughter of Bharmal, Kachwaha ruler of Amer in 1562. He abolished Jaziya tax. He built Ibadat Khana in Fatehpur Sikri in 1575. Introduced Jharokha darshan.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which volume of the 'Akbar-Nama' is known as Ain-i-Akbari and deals with Akbar's administration, household, army and the revenues and geography of his empire? [SSC MTS 05/11/2024]
solution:
(D) Third
Abul Fazl wrote the biography of Akbar 'Akbarnama' in 3 volumes. 1st volume about the ancestors of Akbar, 2nd volume about the reign of Akbar and 3rd volume (Ain-i-Akbari) about the administration of Akbar, which was inspired by Sher Shah Suri's administration. Akbarnama was written in Persian.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which Mughal prince translated the Upanishads into Persian in 1657? [SSC CGL 17/08/2021]
solution:
(B) Dara Shikoh
The eldest son of Shah Jahan, Dara Shikoh translated fifty Upanishads from Sanskrit into Persian in 1657, a work known as 'Sirr-i-Akbar' (the greatest mystery). His most famous work was Majma-ul-Bahrain. He states that the work referred in Quran as the 'Kitab al-maknun' or the hidden book is none other than the Upanishads.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Mughal court chronicles are written in ________. [SSC CGL 20/04/2022]
solution:
(B) Persian
The Mughal court Chronicles were primarily written in Persian language. Akbar promoted Persian as the leading language of his court. Books like Akbarnama, Humayun-nama, Tuzuk-i-jahangiri, Char Chaman and Alamgir-nama by Muhamma Qazim is written in Persian. Baburnama was written by Babur in Turkish but later translated to Persian by Abdul Rahim Khan-i-Khanan.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Consider the following statements in the context of the Mughal Rule: [SSC MTS 28/10/2024]
1. The Mansabdari System was introduced by Akbar in 1571.
2. The Second Battle of Panipat was fought in 1556.
3. Maharana Pratap Singh of Mewar was a contemporary of Babur.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
solution:
(B) Both 1 and 2
The Mansabdari system was introduced by Akbar in 1571. Officer (Mansabdar) was assigned rank (Mansab) on the basis of Zat (Sipahi) and sawar. Lowest rank was 10 and highest was 7000. Mirza Aziz Koka and Raja Man Singh were at 7000 rank. They were paid on the basis of Naqdi (cash) and Jagir. The second Battle of Panipat was fought between Akbar and Hemu (Hindu general of Muhammad Adil Shah) in 1556. Hemu was defeated and slain by Bairam Khan. Maharana Pratap Singh (1540-97) of Mewar was the contemporary of Akbar (1556-1605) and not Babur, who died in 1530.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following crops that spread to northern India in the early years of the 17th century was NOT mentioned in the Ain-i-Akbari in the lists of crops in northern India? [SSC MTS 16/10/2024]
solution:
(B) Tobacco
Tobacco cultivation was introduced in India by Portuguese in 16th century which spread to Northern India around 17th century. Tobacco was not mentioned in Ain-i-Akbari. Crops like Wheat, Jowar, Arhar, Barley, Rice, Cotton, Sugarcane, Indigo were mentioned by Abul Fazl in Ain-i-Akbari.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: As described in 'Ain-i-Akbari' by Abul Fazl-i-Allami, 'gaz' (unit of measuring length) was divided into equal parts called ________. [SSC CGL 20/04/2022]
solution:
(A) Tassuj
As per Ain-i-Akbari by Abul Fazl, Gaz (unit of measuring length) was divided into 24 equal parts, with each part known as 'Tassuj'.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Panch Mahal, a palace in Fatehpur Sikri, was built by the Mughal Emperor ________ [SSC MTS 05/11/2024]
solution:
(C) Akbar
Panch Mahal was a five storey Palace built by Akbar, situated near Harem (Zenana quarters) in Fatehpur Sikri. The pool in front of the Panch Mahal is called the Anoop Talab.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was sent by Akbar to lead the Mughal army in the Battle of Haldighati which was fought in 1576? [SSC MTS 09/10/2024]
solution:
(B) Raja Man Singh
Battle of Haldighati (1576): was fought between Mughal forces led by Raja Man Singh I and against Mewar Kingdom led by Maharana Pratap. Battle was won by Mughals.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Buland Darwaza was the structure built by Akbar at which of the following places in India? [SSC MTS 21/10/2024]
solution:
(B) Fatehpur Sikri
Akbar built the Buland Darwaza (Door of Victory) in Fatehpur Sikri in 1573, to commemorate his victory over Gujarat. It is made of red and buff sandstone, decorated with white and black marble.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: After the ________ of Hakim Mirza, Kabul was annexed by Akbar and given to Raja Man Singh as 'jagir'. [SSC MTS 15/10/2024]
solution:
(A) death
Hakim Mirza was the ruler of Kabul and was Akbar's half brother. He often conflicted with Akbar. In 1581, Akbar led an army to Kabul and defeated him. After death of Hakim Mirza in 1585, Akbar annexed Kabul to the Mughal Empire and granted this territory as jagir to Raja Man Singh.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: ________ in Gujarat was the emporium of western trade during the Mughal Period. [SSC MTS 20/07/2022]
solution:
(D) Surat
Surat was a major trading centre for spices, textiles, precious stones, opium, and other goods. It was located on the coast of Gujarat and was a gateway to trade with West Asia and Europe. Mughal Emperor Jahangir permitted East India Company to establish their factory in Surat in 1613.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: When was Khandesh merged into the Mughal empire by Akbar? [SSC CPO 05/10/2023]
solution:
(B) 1601
Khandesh was ruled by Farooqi dynasty, founded by Malik Ahmad in 1382. Akbar's army captured the Aseergarh Fort in 1601, and completely annexed Khandesh. This was the last victory expedition of Akbar. Akbar died in 1605.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following Mughal Emperors was called Darvesh or a Zinda Fakir? [SSC MTS 22/08/2019]
solution:
(A) Aurangzeb
Aurangzeb was called 'Darvesh' or 'Zinda Fakir' due to his simple lifestyle like a Fakir. He is also known as Alamgir. Aurangzeb died in 1707, his tomb is located at Khuldabad in Aurangabad (Sambhajinagar).

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Which of the following statements about Akbar are true? [SSC CHSL 10/07/2024]
1. He introduced the Mansabdari system.
2. He defeated and merged the state of Bijapur in his empire.
3. He made Fatehpur Sikri his capital.
solution:
(D) Only 1 and 3
Akbar introduced Mansabdari system in 1571. He transferred his capital from Agra to Fatehpur Sikri in 1571. It was Aurangzeb who conquered Bijapur in 1686 and marking the end of Bijapur Sultanate.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Jaimal and Patta were the warriors who were given charge of defending the fort of ________. [SSC CHSL 03/08/2023]
solution:
(D) Chittor
In 1567, Akbar laid siege to the fort of Chittorgarh. It was ruled by Rajputana Kingdom of Mewar, Rana Uday Singh (1537-72). Jaimal and Patta were two Rajput warriors who were given the charge of defending the fort of Chittor against Akbar.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: During the reign of Akbar, ________ was the land that has lain fallow for three or four years. [SSC CGL 21/07/2023]
solution:
(C) Chachar
During the reign of Akbar, land was divided into 4 parts based on Zabti system: Polaj - land cultivated for each crop in succession (best type). Parauti - land that can be left fallow to 1-2 years to recover its strength. Chachar - land that had lain fallow for 3-4 years. Banjar - land uncultivated for 5 years or more.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following cities was the first planned city of the Mughals? [SSC MTS 08/05/2023]
solution:
(B) Fatehpur Sikri
Fatehpur Sikri was built by Akbar in honour of Sheikh Salim Chisti who blessed Akbar with a son. He named his son Salim/Sheikho Baba (Jahangir) in honour of Salim Chisti. Akbar shifted his capital from Agra to Fatehpur Sikri in 1571, from Fatehpur Sikri to Lahore in 1585 and from Lahore to Agra in 1598.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: When was the Battle of Tukaroi fought where Akbar defeated the Sultanate of Bengal and Bihar? [SSC MTS 15/05/2023]
solution:
(C) 1575
Battle of Tukaroi (1575) was fought by Mughal Empire led by Akbar's general Munim Khan and Raja Todar Mal against the Sultanate of Bengal and Bihar led by Daud Khan Karrani. This battle is also known as Battle of Bajhaura or Battle of Mughulmari. This battle resulted in a decisive Mughal victory.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who had built the Badshahi Masjid in Lahore? [SSC MTS 20/08/2019]
solution:
(C) Aurangzeb
The Badshahi Masjid in Lahore was built by the Mughal emperor Aurangzeb between 1671 and 1673. It is an example of Mughal architecture, known for its blend of red sandstone and marble. He also constructed Moti Masjid in Red Fort.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Ain-i Akbari, a document on life of Mughal ruler Akbar does not contain information about ________. [SSC CHSL 21/03/2023]
solution:
(B) Ancestors
Ain-i-Akbari by Abul Fazl does not contain information about Akbar's ancestors. It focuses on his reign, administration, household, army, revenues, and the geography of his empire. The 1st volume of Akbarnama (Abul Fazl) contains information about Akbar's ancestors.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Ajmer became the suba headquarters under the ________. [SSC CGL 03/12/2022]
solution:
(C) Mughals
Ajmer became the suba headquarters under Mughals, after being conquered by Akbar in 1556. Mughal Empire under Akbar was divided into 12 subas: Kabul, Lahore, Multan, Delhi, Agra, Awadh, Allahabad, Bihar, Bengal, Malwa, Ajmer, and Gujarat.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: "Fatwa-i-Alamgiri", a digest of Muslim laws, was written during the period of which of the following kings? [SSC CPO 12/12/2019]
solution:
(B) Aurangzeb
Fatwa-i-Alamgiri, also known as Al-Fatwa-al-Hindiyyah is a Sharia based compilation on statecraft, ethics, justice and punishment, it is the greatest digest of Muslim Law in India.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In which year was Kashmir annexed to the Mughal Empire by Akbar? [SSC PHASE X 01/08/2022]
solution:
(A) 1586
The Mughal conquest of Kashmir was an invasion of the Kashmir Sultanate by the Mughal Empire in 1585-1589. Kashimir was ruled by Yusuf Shah Chak. After severe fighting and heavy casualties, Mughals defeated the Kashmiris in the Battle of Hastivanj (1586).

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The revenue system of the Mughal period during Akbar's reign was called ________. [SSC MTS 26/07/2022]
solution:
(B) Zabti
The Zabti system in Akbar's reign was developed by Raja Todarmal, finance minister. In this system the average production of land for 10 years (Dahsala) is estimated and Zabti, which was 1/3rd of produce was taken.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Military commanders in Akbar's empire were called '________'. [SSC MTS 11/10/2021]
solution:
(A) Faujdars
Faujdars were the military commanders responsible for maintaining law and order. Bakshis - the military paymasters. Diwan - financial officer. Kotwal - Town police commander.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following issued Mahzarnama in 1579? [SSC CGL 23/08/2021]
solution:
(D) Akbar
In 1579, Akbar issued a declaration called Mahzarnama, which granted him authority to be the supreme arbiter or interpreter of Islamic laws, replacing the power of ulema (Islamic Scholars) in religious matters. Mahzar was drafted by Sheikh Mubarak.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which Mughal emperor built the city called 'Makhsudabad' later popularly known as Murshidabad? [SSC CHSL 12/04/2021]
solution:
(C) Akbar
Akbar built the city Makhsudabad in 16th century. In 1704, the nawab of Bengal Murshid Quli Khan transferred his capital from Dacca to Makhsudabad and renamed as Murshidabad. Prominent Nawab of Bengal was Siraj-ud-Daulah.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In the context of Mughal administration, which of the following is the most appropriate definition of 'Abwab'? [SSC CHSL 16/04/2021]

options.a: A clerk in the government treasury.
options.c: A tax levied on the lands over and above the original rent.

solution:
(C) A tax levied on the lands over and above the original rent.
All temporary and circumstantial taxes and impositions levied by the King over and above regular taxes were called Abwabs. Abwabs were imposed in addition to regular land revenue (Zabt) collected by Mughal Empire. It was collected by Zamindars.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Match the monuments with their respective builders. [SSC MTS 30/10/2024]
Monuments | Builders
A. Jama Masjid At Delhi | 1. Babur
B. Allahabad Fort | 2. Sher Shah
C. Quila-i-Kunhan Masjid | 3. Shah Jahan
D. Jama Masjid At Sambal | 4. Akbar
solution:
(B) A-3, B-4, C-2, D-1
Jama Masjid at Delhi - Shah Jahan. Allahabad Fort - Akbar in 1583. Quila i Kunhan Masjid - Sher Shah Suri in Purana Qila (Delhi) in 1541. Jama Masjid at Sambhal - Babur in 1526. Babur gave orders to his general Mir Hindu Beg to build Jama Masjid at Sambhal.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Akbar was succeeded by his son, Salim, who took the title of ________ meaning "Conqueror of the World". [SSC CGL 07/06/2019]
solution:
(B) Jahangir
Jahangir (1605-27 AD): original name - Nur-ud-Din Muhammad Salim/Prince Salim. He established Zanjir-i-Adal (chain of Justice) at Agra, for seekers of royal justice. He married Mehrunnisa (Nur Jahan) and Jagat Gosai or Jodha Bai of Marwar, Rajput princess.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who succeeded in securing trading concessions for the East India Company from Mughal Emperor Jahangir? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 12/10/2023]
solution:
(D) Thomas Roe
In 1615, Thomas Roe, came in as ambassador of King James I of England to court of Jahangir. Jahangir granted him permission and protection for East India Company's factory in Surat. The British established their first factory (temporary factory) at Masulipatnam in 1611 and first permanent factory at Surat in 1613.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Sisodiya Rajput ruler of Mewar, Amar Singh accepted Mughal services during the reign of ________. [SSC CGL 18/07/2023]
solution:
(B) Jahangir
Amar Singh I (1597 - 1620) Sisodiya Rajput ruler of Mewar and son of Maharana Pratap accepted Mughal sovereignty during the reign of Jahangir after prolonged conflict. Mughal-Mewar treaty was signed in 1615 between Amar Singh and Jahangir, also known as Treaty of Chittor. The territories around Chittorgarh, including the fort, were returned to Mewar and Prince Karn Singh of Mewar would attend the Mughal Court. He was given Mansab of 5000.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following wrote the biography of Jahangir known as Iqbalnama-i-Jahangiri? [SSC MTS 09/05/2023]
solution:
(A) Mutamid Khan
Mutamid Khan was military officer under Jahangir who wrote Iqbalnama-i-Jahangiri (biography of jahangir) during the concluding years of his reign. This book is divided into three parts: The first deals with the history of Amir Timur to Humayun. The second focuses on Akbar and the third covers history of Jahangir's reign.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Tuzuk-i-Jahangiri' was originally written in which of the following languages? [SSC CPO 11/11/2022]
solution:
(C) Persian
Tuzuk-i-Jahangiri was the autobiography of Jahangir was written in Persian language. Also known as Jahangirnama. He wrote history of his reign and also mentioned details of art and politics.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Sarai Nurmahal is a centrally protected monument of India. It is situated in which state of India? [SSC MTS 08/07/2022]
solution:
(C) Punjab
Sarai Nurmahal was constructed on the orders of Nur Jahan (wife of Jahangir), under the supervision of Zakariya Khan (governor of the Doab) in 1618 AD.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Mehrunnisa married Emperor Jahangir in ________. [SSC MTS 26/07/2022]
solution:
(C) 1611
Jahangir married to Mehrunnisa (widow of Sher Afghan, a Persian nobleman of Bengal). Later known as Nur Jahan. She was conferred official title of Padshah Begum. She exercised tremendous influence over the state. Jahangir issued silver coins in honour of his wife Mehrunnisa.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who is the author of the book "Tarikh-i-Dilkusha"? [SSC CGL 17/08/2021]
solution:
(B) Bhimsen
Tarikh-i-Dilkusha was written by Bhimsen. It is a memoir of Aurangzeb written in 1707. It was written in Persian and was translated to English by Jadunath Sarkar. Aurangzeb's Deccan campaigns were mentioned in this book.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which city of Pakistan is called the "Gardens of the Mughals or City of Gardens"? [SSC CHSL 09/07/2019]
solution:
(D) Lahore
Lahore, the capital of Punjab (Pakistan) is known as 'Gardens of the Mughals' or 'City of Gardens' due to its rich Mughal heritage and presence of famous Mughal gardens like Shalimar Gardens. Mughal gardens were based on Charbagh style of architecture. Bangalore is known as the Garden City of India.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Captain Hawkins visited India during the reign of: [SSC MTS 16/10/2024]
solution:
(C) Jahangir
In 1608, William Hawkings, representative of East India company arrived in India. He travelled to Agra and visited Jahangir's court in 1609 and was made a Mansab of 400. He sought permission from Jahangir to establish a factory at Surat but was denied. Jahangir called him English Khan.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The mother of Jahangir was a Kachhwaha princess, a daughter of Rajput ruler of ________. [RRB RPF SI 13/12/2024]
solution:
(D) Amber
Jahangir was the son of Akbar and his wife Harka bai, also known as 'Mariam-uz-Zamani'. She was a Kachhawaha princess and the daughter of Rajput ruler of Amber, Raja Bharmal. She was married to Akbar in 1562.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In which year did Prince Salim ascend the Mughal throne? [SSC CPO 23/11/2020]
solution:
(B) 1605
Prince Salim (Jahangir) ascended the throne in 1605. He revolted against his father Akbar during his expedition of Khandesh in 1601. He ruled from 1605 to 1627 and was succeeded by his son Khurram also known as Shah Jahan.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Tomb of Itimad-Ud-Daulah, inlaid with Pietra dura decoration, is located at: [SSC CPO 24/11/2020]
solution:
(C) Agra
The Tomb of Itimad-ud-Daula often called the 'Baby Taj', is a Mughal mausoleum located in Agra and was built by Nur Jahan for her father, Mirza Ghiyas Beg, between 1622 and 1628. It was built of white marble of Rajasthan.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following had been given the title of 'Sultan Buland Iqbal' by Shah Jahan? [SSC CPO 25/11/2020]
solution:
(C) Dara
Shah Jahan's sons - Dara Shikoh, Aurangzeb, Shah Shuja and Murad Baksh. Shah Jahan formally confirmed Dara Shikoh (eldest son) as his heir and granted him the title of 'Sultan Buland Iqbal' (King of High Fortune) in 1642. Dara was a known poet, scholar and Sufi mystic. His famous book is 'Majma-ul-Bahrain' means, the confluence of two oceans.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following administrative units was introduced during the reign of Shahjahan? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 11/12/2024]
solution:
(B) Chakla
The administrative unit 'Chakla' was introduced during the reign of Shah Jahan. It consists of a group of parganas (smaller divisions) within the Mughal Empire.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following has written the 'Badshah Nama'? [SSC CGL TIER-II 26/10/2023]
solution:
(B) Abdul Hamid Lahori
Padshahnama (Badshahnama) was written by Abdul Hamid Lahori during the reign of Shah Jahan. It was written in Persian language. Lahori wrote two volumes to this book about the first 20 years of Shah Jahan's reign from 1627-1647 AD.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The mausoleum of the Taj Mahal was designed by ________. [SSC MTS 12/09/2023]
solution:
(D) Ustad Ahmad Lahori
The Taj Mahal was built by Shah Jahan in the memory of his wife Mumtaz Mahal (Arzumand Bano Begum), who died in 1631. It was built from 1632 to 1653, it was completed in 1648 but the mosque, guest house, the main gateway and its cloisters were added and completed in 1653. The Architect/Designer of Taj Mahal was Ustad Ahmad Lahori. UNESCO site in 1983.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The art of decoration, called Pietra dura became popular during whose reign? [SSC CGL 19/07/2023]
solution:
(C) Shah Jahan
Pietra dura, an inlay technique using cut and polished coloured stones to create images and floral designs. It became popular during Shah Jahan's reign. Taj Mahal, Jama Masjid of Delhi, tomb of Itimad-ud-Daula are prime examples of Pietra dura. It was also known as 'Prachin Kari'.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following Mughal emperors built Sheesh Mahal in Agra? [SSC MTS 09/05/2023]
solution:
(A) Shah Jahan
The Sheesh Mahal in Agra fort was built by Shah Jahan. Wide use of mirrors was done on both walls and ceilings. Abdul Hamid Lahori mentions that these mirrors were brought from Haleb (Aleppo, Syria) for this purpose and he also referred to this structure as 'Shishaye Halebi'.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following had written the book 'Char Chaman', during the reign of Shah Jahan, describing the Mughal nobility? [SSC CGL 16/08/2021]
solution:
(A) Chandrabhan Brahman
Char Chaman, which describes the Mughals during the reign of Shah Jahan was written by Chandrabhan Brahman. He was a poet of Persian Language and served Mughal court from 1630 to 1660. He wrote Char Chaman into four chapters.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following Mughal Princes allied with Malik Ambar and revolted against his father? [RRB RPF SI 09/12/2024]
solution:
(C) Shah Jahan
Malik Ambar was the Prime Minister and regent of Ahmednagar Sultanate from 1600 until his death in 1626. Jahangir considered him as his arch-nemesis. Ambar assisted Shah Jahan in revolt against his father. Nur Jahan (stepmother of Shah Jahan) had ambitions of seating her son-in-law on the throne, which led to Shah Jahan's rebellion.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Red Fort and the Jama Masjid in Delhi stand out as towering achievements of architecture during the reign of ________. [SSC CGL 07/06/2019]
solution:
(A) Shah Jahan
Red Fort also known as Lal Qila in Delhi was built by Shah Jahan in 1639 using red sandstone. Jama Masid of Delhi (Masjid-i-jehan-Numa) was built by Shah Jahan in 1656, in the city of Shahjahanabad.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which State was founded by Nizam-ul-Mulk in 1724? [SSC CPO 27/06/2024]
solution:
(B) Hyderabad
Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah I also known as Chin Qilich Qamaruddin Khan founded the state of Hyderabad in 1724 and became the first Nizam of Hyderabad. It was ruled by Nizams for nearly 2 centuries and was integrated into Indian Union in 1948 through Operation Polo.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Prince Khurram grew up to be known as: [SSC CPO 16/03/2019]
solution:
(C) Shah Jahan
Shah Jahan (1628-58 AD): also known as 'Khurram'. Son of Jahangir and Jodha Bai/Jagat Gosai (daughter of Raja Uday Singh). Defeated Portuguese in 1632. Kingdoms of Ahmednagar, Bijapur and Golconda accepted his suzerainty in 1637. He built Diwan-i-aam (common people) and Diwan-i-Khas (for King and nobles) in Red Fort, Delhi. He placed Peacock Throne in Diwan-i-Khas which was decorated with Kohinoor diamond.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In which of the following years was the battle of Bannihatti fought? [RRB RPF SI 09/12/2024]
solution:
(C) 1565
Battle of Bannihatti also known as Battle of Talikota or Rakshasi Tangadi, was fought between Vijayanagar Empire against alliance of Deccan Sultanates (Bijapur, Golconda, Ahmadnagar and Bidar) in 1565, resulting in defeat of Vijayanagar kingdom (led by Aliya Rama Raya).

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Under whose rule did the Mughal empire achieve its largest territorial limits? [SSC CPO 27/06/2024]
solution:
(B) Aurangzeb
Aurangzeb (1658 - 1707): original name - Muhi al-Din Muhammad, 6th Mughal emperor. During his reign Mughal Empire achieved largest territorial limits spanning nearly the entire Indian Subcontinent. He was the longest reigning Mughal Emperor. He re-introduced jaziya tax which was abolished by Akbar and imposed Sharia law as per Islam.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was the successor of Mughal emperor Shahjahan to the throne? [SSC CPO 04/10/2023]
solution:
(C) Aurangzeb
In 1657, Shah Jahan's failing health set off the war of succession among his sons. Shah Jahan wanted Dara Shikoh to ascend the throne but Aurangzeb defeated all his sons and ascended the throne in 1658. He imprisoned his father Shah Jahan in the Agra fort, where he died in captivity in 1666 and was buried in Taj Mahal near Mumtaz's tomb.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Under whose reign was the maximum number of books in Persian on classical Indian Music written? [SSC CGL 20/07/2023]
solution:
(D) Aurangzeb
Aurangzeb had orthodox Islamic views and he banned music, singing and dancing in his court in 1668. He didn't ban musical instruments except pungi. He himself used to play veena. His reign saw a significant number of Persian works on Indian Classical music.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which Mughal Emperor has attempted to vandalise the Kailash Temple at Ellora? [SSC MTS 02/05/2023]
solution:
(A) Aurangzeb
In 1682, Aurangzeb sent a large workforce around 1000 people to demolish Kailasa Temple at Ellora. Despite working for three years, the workforce could manage to disfigure some statues and unable to fully destroy the temple.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following Mughal emperors assumed the title Alamgir, World Conqueror? [SSC MTS 10/05/2023]
solution:
(B) Aurangzeb
Aurangzeb took the title of 'Alamgir'. He was a follower of the Mujaddidi and a disciple of Punjabi saint, Ahmad Sirhindi. During the first 23 years of his rule from 1658-81 Aurangzeb concentrated in North India. The next 25 years he spent fighting with Marathas in South.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Bijapur was annexed by Aurangzeb in which year? [SSC CPO 09/11/2022]
solution:
(B) 1686
In 1685, Aurangzeb dispatched his son, Muhammad Azam Shah, to capture Bijapur Fort and defeat Sikandar Adil Shah (ruler of Bijapur). Aurangzeb himself arrived in September 1686 and commanded the siege of Bijapur.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was defeated by Aurangzeb in the Battle of Deorai? [SSC MTS 20/10/2021]
solution:
(C) Dara Shikoh
Battle of Deorai (1659) - between Dara Shikoh and Aurangzeb. Last battle between them. Dara Shikoh was captured and executed after this battle. Battle of Dharmat (1658) - fought by Aurangzeb against Jaswant Singh Rathore and Dara Shikoh. Aurangzeb won a decisive victory. Battle of Samugarh (1658) - second battle fought between Dara Shikoh and his three brothers Aurangzeb, Shah Shuja and Murad to decide the heir of the throne. Aurangzeb won this decisive battle.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar died in ________. [SSC MTS 25/07/2022]
solution:
(C) 1862
Bahadur Shah II with his wife Begum Zeenat Mahal was captured and exiled by British in Rangoon (Yangoon) in 1858 where he died in 1862 and was buried in Yangoon.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who was the Mughal emperor during the third battle of Panipat? [SSC CGL 24/07/2023]
solution:
(B) Shahalam II
Third Battle of Panipat was fought on 14 Jan 1761 between Maratha Confederacy led by Sadashivrao Bhau and Afghans (Durrani) led by Ahmad Shah Durrani (Ahmad Shah Abdali). Marathas were defeated in this battle.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following Khayal singers of the 19th century was given the title of "Tanras" by Bahadur Shah Jaffar, the last Mughal Emperor of India? [SSC CHSL 27/05/2022]
solution:
(B) Meer Qutub Baksh
Meer Qutub Baksh (Tanrus Khan) was an iconic Khayal singer of 19th century. He was the founder of the Delhi Gharana of Hindustani Music. He was a musician in the court of Bahadur Shah Zafar.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following statements is INCORRECT about Bibi ka Maqbara at Aurangabad? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 11/12/2024]

options.a: It is built completely with marble.
options.b: It was built by Mughal prince Azam Shah.
options.c: It is the burial place of Aurangzeb's wife, Rabia-ud-Durrani.
options.d: It is an imitation of the Taj Mahal due to its similar designs.

solution:
(A) It is built completely with marble.
Bibi ka Maqbara is a tomb of Dilras Banu Begum (also known as Rabia-ud-Durrani), wife of Aurangzeb. Built in 1660 by Aurangzeb's son, Prince Azam Shah in memory of his mother. It is an imitation of the Taj Mahal and also called 'Taj of Deccan' or 'Dakkhani Taj'. It was designed and erected by Ata-ullah (son of Ustad Ahmad Lahori, who designed Taj Mahal) and Hanspat Rai.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which Mughal ruler was responsible for the execution of Guru Tegh Bahadur in the year 1675? [SSC CHSL 11/07/2024]
solution:
(B) Aurangzeb
Guru Tegh Bahadur was the son of Guru Hargobind, sixth Sikh Guru. He was born in 1621 and was named Tyaga mal at birth. He became ninth Sikh guru in 1664. On 24 November 1675, he was publicly beheaded on the orders of Aurangzeb.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In which of the following years was the state of Golconda finally annexed into the Mughal empire? [SSC MTS 11/11/2024]
solution:
(B) 1687
The siege of Golconda was an eight month military siege of the Golconda fort in 1687 by Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb against the Golconda Sultanate ruled by King Abul Hasan Qutb Shah of Qutb Shahi dynasty. Mughals completely annexed Golconda and ended the Qutb Shahi rule.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The second largest dome in the world was built in India and is still standing strong. Which dome is being spoken about here? [SSC MTS 04/11/2024]
solution:
(A) Gol Gumbaz
Gol Gumbaz is a 17th century mausoleum located in Bijapur, Karnataka. The tombs of Sultans of Adil Shahi dynasty (Bijapur part of Bahmani Kingdom) are placed here. It was added to UNESCO site in 2014 under the name Monuments and Forts of the Deccan Sultanate.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following signed the Treaty of Allahabad in August 1765 with the Nawab of Awadh and Shah Alam II, the Mughal Emperor? [SSC MTS 12/11/2024]
solution:
(B) Robert Clive
The treaty of Allahabad was signed on 16 August 1765 between Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II and Robert Clive of East India Company. It was signed after the Battle of Buxar (1764). In this treaty, the Nizamat functions (administration) were given to Shah Alam II and Diwani functions (revenue collection) to East India Company.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Consider the following statements: [CAPF 2021]
1. Under Akbar, the system of Zabt was established over a large area from Punjab to Awadh.
2. In the seventeenth century North India, the system of Zabt lost much ground to the system of Kankut.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
solution:
(C) Both 1 and 2
Zabt system was in practise during reign of Sher Shah Suri. Perfectly implemented by Raja Todarmal during Akbar's rule. Also known as Dahsala system. Based on average produce of crops for a period of 10 years. Applied mainly in Delhi, Punjab, Awadh, Agra, Lahore, Punjab. Kankut system (Danabandi system) was a land revenue assessment system. 'Kan' means grain and 'Kut' means estimate. Grain yield was estimated through past payment. It was also introduced during Mughals. By the 17th century North India, Zabt system declined in many areas due to administrative difficulties and the Kankut system regained prominence.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Which of the following statements about Akbar are correct? [CAPF 2021]
1. In 1560s, Akbar had the Fort of Agra constructed.
2. In 1570s, Akbar decided to build a new capital at Fatehpur Sikri.
3. Akbar also commissioned a white marble tomb of Shaikh Salim Chishti at Sikri.
4. In 1585, Akbar decided to shift the capital back to Agra.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
solution:
(B) 1, 2 and 3
Mughal emperor Akbar initiated the construction of the Agra fort in 1560s. In 1571, Akbar built a new planned city of Fatehpur Sikri and shifted his capital from Agra to Fatehpur Sikri in 1571, Fatehpur Sikri to Lahore in 1585 and Lahore to Agra in 1598. Shaikh Salim Chisti blessed Akbar with a son who was called Salim in his honour. Akbar commissioned a white marble tomb of Salim Chisti near the Jama Masjid in Fatehpur Sikri.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which one of the following painters was not associated with Humayun? [CDS I 2021]
solution:
(D) Bihzad
After Humayun returned from Persia (where he took refuge till 1555 AD), he brought back two painters Mir Sayyid Ali (a pupil of Bihzad) and Abdus Samad. These two artists played a crucial role in developing the Mughal school of painting under Akbar. Maulana Dost Musawir and Maulana Yusuf were also painters in Humayun's court. Bihzad (Kamal-ud-din Bihzad) was a famous Persian miniaturist and painter from Herat (Timurid Court) but never worked under Humayun.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Akbar issued a Farman in 1598 permitting in the city of Cambay (Khambat), Gujarat, the construction of a ________ [CDS II 2021]
solution:
(B) Church
Akbar issued a Farman in 1598 allowing development of a Church in the town of Cambay (Khambat), Gujarat. He permitted the Jesuit order when Christian Saints (Father) visited his court in 1598. He ordered the officials of Cambay (Khambat) to not oppose them in building the Church.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Which of the following terms were used in the Indo-Persian sources of the Mughal period to denote a peasant? [CDS I 2022]
1. Raiyat
2. Asami
3. Muzarian
4. Majur
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
solution:
(C) 1, 2 and 3 only
The terms which Indo-Persian sources of the Mughal period most frequently used to denote peasants was Raiyat (or Riya) or Muzarian and Kisan or Asami. There were two kinds of peasants Khud-Kashta, residents of same village in which held their land and Pahi-Kashta, peasants who belong to other village and cultivate land elsewhere on contractual basis.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Man Kautuhal, a work on music, was prepared under the aegis of [CDS I 2024]
solution:
(A) Raja Man Singh of Gwalior
'Man Kutuhal' is a work on music and dance. It covers wide range of topics, including the history of music and theory of music. Raja Man Singh Tomar was the ruler of Gwalior from 1486 to 1516. The musical form Dhrupad remained a strong point of the Gwalior gharana for many centuries.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which one of the following was the homeland of Babur? [CISF AC 2021]
solution:
(A) Farghana
In 1494, Babur ascended the throne of Farghana, a state in Uzbekistan. He was a Timurid Prince. The Uzbek chief, Shaibani Khan defeated Babur and captured Samarqand. This forced Babur to move towards Kabul which he conquered in 1504.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: With reference to medieval India, which one of the following is the correct sequence in ascending order in terms of size? [IAS 2021]
solution:
(A) Paragana-Sarkar-Suba
The entire Kingdom was divided into Suba or Pranta governed by Subedar, Suba was divided into Sarkar led by Faujdar. Then Sarkar was divided into Pargana led by Siqdar and finally Pargana was divided into villages, under the governance of Muquaddam.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: "Yogavasistha" was translated into Persian by Nizamuddin Panipati during the reign of: [IAS 2022]
solution:
(A) Akbar
Akbar established translation department (Makhtab Khana) around 1575 at Fatehpur Sikri and attached a royal library to it. Large number of Sanskrit texts including Ramayana and Mahabharata were translated into Persian. Yogavasishtha is a philosophical text composed in Sanskrit by Sage Valmiki. It is a dialogue between the Sage Vasistha and Prince Rama. It was translated to Persian by Akbar's courtier Nizamuddin Panipati also known as Mir Bakshi.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In medieval India, the process of indigo extraction underwent great improvement as a result of the: [CDS I 2025]

options.a: use of fertilizer enhancing the quality of indigo crop.
options.b: Introduction of mechanical crushing tools for indigo stems.
options.c: use of gypsum and lime mortars in making the inner surfaces of indigo vats water-proof.
options.d: employment of slave labour in quick desilting of indigo crucibles.

solution:
(C) use of gypsum and lime mortars in making the inner surfaces of indigo vats water-proof.
The process of Indigo extraction underwent improvement due to the use of gypsum and lime mortars to make the inner surfaces of indigo vats water-proof, it prevented leakages and contamination, allowing for better control over the fermentation and extraction stages.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which one of the following is listed in the Ain-i Akbari as one of the 'Indian sciences' prescribed by Akbar for scholars to learn at school? [CDS I 2025]
solution:
(D) Jyotisha
Abul Fazl was the court poet of Akbar. He wrote biography of Akbar, Akbarnama in 3 parts, first about ancestors of Akbar, second about reign of Akbar and third is Ain-i-Akbari, about administration of Akbar. In Ain-i-Akbari, Abul Fazl mentions that Akbar prescribed scholars to learn Indian sciences like Jyotisha, Mimansa, Patanjali etc. Answer may vary as per official answer key.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Twenty five percent of the land revenue collected by Marathas in the Deccan was ________. [SSC MTS 02/05/2023]
solution:
(C) Chauth
Marathas were granted the right to collect Chauth and Sardeshmukhi by the Mughals in Deccan region in exchange for a promise to not raid adjoining Mughal territories. Chauth or Chauthai (1/4th) was an annual tax nominally levied at 25% on revenue or produce.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following Anglo-Maratha wars resulted in merging the Peshwas' territories with the Bombay Presidency? [SSC CHSL 02/08/2023]
solution:
(B) Third
Third Anglo Maratha War (1817 - 1818): often regarded as Pindari War. Final war between the British led by Governor-General Hastings, General Hislop and the Maratha Confederacy. Baji Rao II captured and sent to Bithoor in 1818. Treaty of Mandsaur was signed. The Maratha territories were merged with Bombay presidency and Peshwaship was abolished.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Baji Rao I or Baji Rao Ballal was the son of ________. [SSC MTS 11/05/2023]
solution:
(A) Peshwa Balaji Vishwanath
Peshwa Balaji Vishwanath (1713 - 1720): first Peshwa or PM of Shahu. He was given title 'Sena Karte' (maker of the army) by Shahu in 1708. In 1719, Sayyid brothers with the help of Balaji Vishwanath defeated Farrukhsiyar.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was a famous ruler of the Maratha dynasty? [SSC CHSL 13/03/2023]
solution:
(B) Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj
Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj (1674 - 1680 AD): born at Shivneri Fort in 1630. Father - Shahji Bhosle. Mother - Jija Bai. Religious teacher - Samarth Ramdas. He defeated Afzal Khan in Battle of Pratapgarh (1659). Defeated Shaista khan in 1663 in Pune. Escaped from Agra Fort in 1666. Shivaji coronated in 1674 at Raigarh and assumed the title of 'Chhatrapati' and Haindava Dharmodharak (Protector of Hinduism).

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Council of Ministers during the time of Shivaji Maharaj was known as: [SSC CGL 07/03/2020]
solution:
(C) Ashta Pradhan
Ashta Pradhan was established by Shivaji in 1674, when he was crowned. Ashta Pradhans (8 ministers): Peshwa (Prime Minister), Amatya/Mazumdar (Finance), Sachiv (Secretary), Waqia-Navis (Interior), Sar-i-Naubar/Senapati (Commander-in-Chief), Sumant/Dabir (Foreign), Nyayadhashish (Judge), Panditrao (High Priest).

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following pairs is associated with the taxation system of the Marathas? [SSC CGL 06/06/2020]
solution:
(A) Chauth and Sardeshmukhi
Chauth and Sardeshmukhi functioned in Mughal Provinces of Deccan and were levied by Marathas in 18th century. Chauth was tax in which 1/4th or 25% of the revenue to be paid to Marathas for not being subjected to Maratha raids. Sardeshmukhi was the additional tax of 10% over Chauth levied on large land owners.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In which of the following cities is the Mahakaleshwar temple situated? [RRB NTPC CBT-I 31/07/2021]
solution:
(C) Ujjain
The Mahakaleshwar Temple of Ujjain was destroyed by Iltutmish in in 1234. It was reconstructed by Ramchandra Baba Sukthankar, Maratha Diwan of Ranoji Shinde.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: ________ became the capital of the Maratha Empire after the Peshwas won over Shivaji. [SSC MTS 21/08/2019]
solution:
(D) Poona
Capital during Chhatrapati Shivaji was Raigad Fort. Bajirao I, shifted the administrative capital from Satara (Capital after Raigad) to Pune (Poona) in 1728 AD. Shaniwar Wada, a historical fort was built in Pune and was the seat of Peshwas until 1818.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was killed by Chatrapati Shivaji in the Battle of Pratapgarh? [SSC CHSL 04/07/2019]
solution:
(A) Afzal Khan
Battle of Pratapgarh (1659): this battle was fought between Maratha forces led by Shivaji and the Bijapur troops under General Afzal Khan. The Marathas emerged victorious.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: As per the treaty of Purandar, how many forts were surrendered by Shivaji to Mughals? [SSC MTS 17/10/2024]
solution:
(D) 23
The treaty of Purandar (1665) was signed between Jai Singh of Amber and Shivaji. Shivaji agreed to give up 23 of his forts out of 35 to Mughals.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Chhatrapati Shivaji and Soyarabai named their son ________. [SSC CPO 15/03/2019]
solution:
(D) Rajaram
Rajaram (1689 - 1700): succeeded the throne with the help of ministers at Raigad. He was the son of Shivaji and Soyarabai (2nd wife of Shivaji) and was the younger brother of Sambhaji. He created a new post 'Pratinidhi' to Ashta Pradhans, now total 9 ministers. He died at Satara, it became the capital after the fall of Jinji to Mughals in 1698.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Shivaji's son from Saibai was named ________. [SSC CPO 16/03/2019]
solution:
(A) Sambhaji
Sambhaji (1680 - 1689): elder son of Shivaji from Saibai. Defeated Rajaram his younger brother. Provided protection and support to Mirza Akbar, rebellious son of Aurangzeb, due to which Aurangzeb gave him gruesome death.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Third battle of Panipat was fought between ________. [SSC CGL 01/12/2022]
solution:
(C) Marathas and Afghans
Third Battle of Panipat (1761): was fought during the reign of Balaji Baji Rao also known as Nana Saheb (1740 - 1761). It was one of the deadliest wars in the world. Peshwa Sadashivrao Bhau led the Maratha army and Ahmad Shah Abdali (Durrani Empire) led the Afghan Forces. The Marathas were defeated by the Afghans.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following dances of Goa is performed to celebrate the victory of the Maratha warriors over the Portuguese? [SSC MTS 01/10/2024]
solution:
(D) Ghodemodni
Ghode Modni dance is a vibrant Goan folk dance, celebrated during the Shigmo Festival. 'Ghode' means horse and 'modni' refers to 'dance'. It is performed to celebrate the victory of Maratha warriors who defended the Goa from Portuguese.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In 1818, the Holkar Dynasty signed the Treaty of Mandsaur with the ________. [SSC MTS 27/10/2021]
solution:
(C) British
Treaty of Mandsaur was signed in 1818 between the British Sir Thomas Hislop and the Marathas Malhar Rao Holkar. It was signed after the Battle of Mahidpur, the Holkars were defeated and accepted the British suzerainty.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Which of the following statements is/are correct? [SSC MTS 22/10/2024]
I. Balshastri Jambhekar started the first Anglo-Maratha newspaper - 'Durpun', in 1832.
II. He is remembered as the father of Marathi journalism.
III. Balshastri Jambhekar published the first Marathi monthly magazine - 'Digdurshun' in 1840.
solution:
(C) I, II and III
Balshastri Jambhekar (1812 - 1846) was an Indian Journalist and social reformer from Bombay Presidency. He established 'Darpan' the first Anglo-Marathi newspaper in 1832. He started Digdarshan, the first Marathi monthly magazine. He is considered as the 'Father of Marathi Journalism'.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which Peshwa signed the treaty of Bassein with the East India Company? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 12/10/2023]
solution:
(C) Baji Rao II
Treaty of Bassein (1802) was a pact between British East India Company and Baji Rao II, Peshwa of Marathas. It was signed after the Battle of Poona (1802) where Peshwa Baji Rao II and Scindias were defeated by Yashwantrao Holkar, ruler of Indore. Treaty of Bassein led to the Second Anglo-Maratha War (1803 - 1805) which was won by the British.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Which of the following statements is/are correct? [CDS II 2023]
1. Shivaji sent an ambassador to Bombay to negotiate an agreement with the English.
2. His chief motive was to secure English aid against the Siddis of Danda-Rajpuri.
3. He wanted a supply of grenades, mortars and other ammunition.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
solution:
(D) 1, 2 and 3
Shivaji sent an ambassador to Bombay in 1671 to negotiate an agreement with the British. To secure British support against the Siddis of Danda-Rajpuri. Danda-Rajpuri was the control centre of Siddi's naval contingent, comprises of large ships. Shivaji wanted a supply of grenades, mortars and other ammunitions from the British to strengthen his forces.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Which naval forts were erected by Shivaji? [CAPF 2024]
1. Suvarnadurg
2. Vijayadurg
3. Sindhudurg
4. Kolaba
Select the answer using the code given below:
solution:
(A) 1, 2, 3 and 4
Shivaji Maharaj built Suvarnadurg in 1660 AD. It is located on an island off the coast of Maharashtra near town Dapoli. Shivaji built Vijayadurga Fort in 1653 AD on the Konkan coast. Shivaji built Sindhudurga Fort in 1657 AD on a small island of Malwan in Sindhudurga district. Shivaji built Kolaba Fort (Alibaug Fort) in 1680, for protection of Maratha ships against the Portuguese and the British.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Consider the following statements about the administrative measures of Shivaji: [CDS II 2024]
1. The scheme of Ashta Pradhan (eight ministers) was completed and announced at the time of Shivaji's coronation with regulations and duties properly defined.
2. Shivaji introduced Marathi in place of Persian and coined Sanskrit technical terms for administrative purposes.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
solution:
(C) Both 1 and 2
Shivaji Maharaj introduced a new 'Ashta Pradhan' system during the time of coronation in 1674 at Raigad Fort. Before Shivaji's reign, Persian was the dominant language. Shivaji made Marathi the official language of his administration and coined Sanskrit terms like Chhatrapati, Peshwa, Mantri, Nyayadhish, Amatya etc.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Which of the following statements about the Bundela rebellion against Aurangzeb are correct? [CAPF 2024]
1. Shivaji advised Chhatrasal to promote local risings against Aurangzeb.
2. Chhatrasal levied chauth like marathas.
3. In 1705, Aurangzeb decided to make peace with Chhatrasal and Chhatrasal was granted mansab of four-thousand.
Select the answer using the code given below:
solution:
(D) 1, 2 and 3
Maharaja Chhatrasal Bundela belonged to Bundela Rajputs, he founded Bundelkhand Kingdom and ruled from 1670 - 1731. Shivaji encouraged Chhatrasal to mobilize local resistance and using guerrilla tactics against Mughals. Chhatrasal adopted the Chauth system, similar to Marathas. In 1705, Aurangzeb granted Chhatrasal a Mansab of 4000, offering him complete autonomy within Mughal system over his rule in Bundelkhand.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Arrange the following events associated with Shivaji in chronological order: [CDS II 2023]
1. Attack on Afzal Khan
2. Capture of Torna
3. Attack on Shaista Khan
4. Annexation of Javli
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
solution:
(A) 2, 4, 1, 3
Shivaji captured Torna in 1646, it was first conquest made by Shivaji. He annexed Javli in 1656 from Chandra Rao More, sardar of Adilshahi sultanate of Bijapur. Defeated Afzal Khan in Battle of Pratapgarh (1659). Shivaji attacked Shaista Khan in 1663 at his camp at Lal Mahal, Pune.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following pairs of years and historical events is correctly matched? [CAPF 2022]
solution:
(A) 1878: Second Anglo-Afghan War
Second Anglo-Afghan War (1878 - 1880) - between British Empire and the Emirate of Afghanistan ruled by Sher Ali Khan. Treaty of Gandamak was signed in 1879. Britishers won the battle. The Ilbert Bill controversy happened in 1883. Fox's India bill was introduced by Charles James Fox in 1783.
