Lord Curzon became the Viceroy of India in 1899, succeeding Lord Elgin, and served until 1905.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who was the teacher of the famous musician Tansen? [SSC CPO 04/10/2023]
solution:
(D) Swami Haridas
Swami Haridas was an Indian spiritual poet and classical musician. He was a court musician of Raja Man Singh Tomar of Gwalior. He was an expert of Dhrupad Raag. Founder of Haridasi school of mysticism.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Name the Bhakti Saint from South India who was initially a Jaina and a minister in the court of a Chalukya king in the twelfth century. [SSC CGL 27/07/2023]
solution:
(B) Basavanna
Basavanna (1106 - 1168): prominent figure of Bhakti movement in Karnataka. Founded Lingayat / Virshaiva sect. He was initially a Jain and served as a minister in the court of a Chalukya King. He was a believer of Lord Shiva. He was against caste system and vedic rituals.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Bhakti saints, Nayanars were devotees of which of the following gods? [SSC MTS 20/06/2023]
solution:
(B) Shiva
The Nayanars were a group of 63 Tamil saint-poets who were devoted to Lord Shiva. They lived between 6th and 9th centuries AD. They expressed their devotion to Shiva through hymns, poetry, teachings, forming the foundation of Shaivism in Tamil Nadu.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was the founder of the Bhakti sect named 'Pushtimarg'? [SSC MTS 20/10/2021]
solution:
(A) Vallabhacharya
Vallabhacharya (1479 - 1531): gave Shuddhadvaita philosophy. He gave the philosophy of 'Pushti Marg' (Path of Grace) also called Vallabh Sampradaya. He was a devotee of Lord Krishna.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following is NOT a correct match of Dargah and its respective location? [SSC CHSL 06/08/2021]

options.b: Khwaja Qutbuddin Bakhtiyar Kaki Dargah - Delhi

solution:
(A) Shaikh Nizamuddin Auliya Dargah - Agra
Sheikh Nizamuddin Auliya dargah is in Delhi. He was a revered Sufi saint of Chishti Order and popularly known as Mahbub-i-Ilahi. Khwaja Qutubuddin Bakhtiyar Kaki, his dargah is in Delhi. Qutub Minar was named after him. Haji Ali Dargah was constructed in Mumbai, in the memory of Sayyed Pir Haji Ali Shah Bukhari.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Wadali Brothers are ________ singers. [SSC CPO 04/10/2023]
solution:
(C) Sufi
The Wadali brothers, Puranchand Wadali and Pyarelal Wadali are Sufi singers and musicians from Guru Ki Wadali in Amritsar (Punjab). Government of India awarded Padam Shri to Ustad Puranchand Wadali in 2005.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Nooran Sisters are renowned for which of the following genres of Indian Music? [SSC CHSL 24/05/2022]
solution:
(B) Sufi
The Nooran Sisters, Sultana and Jyoti Nooran are Sufi singers. They perform Sham Chaurasia gharana classical music. Qawwali is a form of devotional Sufi music introduced by Amir Khusrau in 13th century. Ghazal is a form of Arabic poetry. Hindustani classical is a form of music practiced in northern parts of India.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Sufi shrine Charar-e-Sharief (or Sharif) is located near ________ city. [SSC CHSL 05/08/2021]
solution:
(A) Srinagar
Charar-e-Sharief is a resting place dedicated to Sheikh-ul-Alam or Sheikh Noor-ud-Din Norrani also known as 'Nund Rishi'. It is located near Srinagar in Budgam district of J&K.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Shaikh Muinuddin Sijzi (Moinuddin Chishti) dargah is situated in which of the following cities? [SSC CHSL 15/04/2021]
solution:
(B) Ajmer
Sheikh Moinuddin Sijzi/Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti (1141 - 1236) dargah also known as Ajmer Sharif, located in Ajmer. He was founder of Chishti order in India and was a part of Sufi movement.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following Sikh Gurus laid the foundation of 'Khalsa Panh'? [SSC CHSL 19/04/2021]
solution:
(A) Guru Gobind Singh
Guru Gobind Singh (1675 - 1708): he established Khalsa Panth on 13 April 1699 on Baisakhi. He wrote 'Zafarnama'. He was the last Sikh guru in human form and he passed the Guruship to the Guru Granth Sahib (holy scripture). He was assassinated in 1708 by two Afghans sent by Wazir Khan (Mughal ruler of Sirhind).

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In the context of Sufi traditions what is the meaning of the word 'sama'? [SSC MTS 05/10/2021]
solution:
(A) recitation of sacred songs
Sama refers to the recitation of holy songs, poetry, and prayer often accompanied by music, dance and other rituals, as a form of remembrance and connection with the divine.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In the context of medieval Sufi tradition, the term "wali" meant? [SSC CPO 12/12/2019]
solution:
(D) Saint
In Sufism, Wali is a friend of God or a Saint. He is believed to have achieved a high level of spiritual enlightenment. Other words: Tasawwuf - Sufism, Sheikh/Pir/Murshid - spiritual teacher, Murid - Disciple, Khalifah - successor, Khanqah - the hospice (residential place), Sama - musical recital, Raksa - dance, Fana - self-annihilation, Ziyarat - pilgrimage to the tombs of Sufi Saints.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In 1590, the Govind Deva temple of Vrindavan. Near Mathura was constructed from ________ sandstone? [SSC MTS 26/07/2022]
solution:
(D) Red
Govind Deva Temple of Vrindavan was built by Raja Man Singh of Amber during 1590 AD. It was built using red sandstone. It is a Vaishnavite temple dedicated to Lord Krishna.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Lal Ded, a female saint of the 14th century, was one of the earliest and best-known ________ poetesses? [SSC MTS 22/10/2021]
solution:
(C) Kashmiri
Lad Ded (1320 - 1392), also known as Lalleshwari or Lal Arifa. She was an Indian Kashmiri mystic of the Kashmir Shaivism school of Hindu philosophy. She was the creator of the style of mystic poetry called Vatsun or Vakhs, also known as Lal Vakhs.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following first translated the "Bhagavad Gita" into English? [SSC CHSL 09/07/2019]
solution:
(D) Charles Wilkins
The first english translation of 'Bhagavad Gita' was done by Charles Wilkins in 1785, his translation is known as 'Wilkin's Gita'.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In 19th century, the Satnami movement in central India was founded by ________. [SSC CHSL 10/07/2019]
solution:
(D) Guru Ghasidas
The Satnami movement was founded by Guru Ghasidas in Chhattisgarh region in 1820. He worked among the leather workers and organised a movement to improve social status of their caste.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The leading light of the Rama cult was saint-poet ________ who also wrote the poem "Ramacharitamanasa". [SSC CGL 07/06/2019]
solution:
(C) Tulsidas
Tulsidas (1532 - 1623): was a revered Indian poet and leading light of Rama cult. He was born into a Brahmin family, and his birth name was Rambola. He wrote 'Ramacharitamanasa' in Awadhi language and also wrote Hanuman Chalisa, Dohavali, Kavitavali and Vinaya Patrika.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: After the death of Shri Guru Gobind Singh, the Sikhs revolted against the ________ under the leadership of Banda Bahadur. [SSC CGL 19/04/2022]
solution:
(B) Mughals
Guru Gobind Singh, 10th Sikh Guru, was killed by the assassins sent by Wazir Khan in 1708. He was succeeded by Banda Bahadur in 1710. Banda Bahadur defeated Mughals in the Battle of Chappar Chiri (1710). Later, he was captured and murdered by Mughal forces under Farrukhsiyar in 1716.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Pattachitra art form is dedicated to which Lord in Hindu mythology? [SSC CGL 13/08/2021]
solution:
(A) Lord Jagannath
Pattachitra art form, a traditional cloth-based scroll painting from Odisha. It is dedicated to Lord Jagannath, an incarnation of Lord Krishna (Vishnu). It depicts scenes from the Jagannath Rath Yatra of Odisha, featuring Lord Jagannath, Balabhadra and Subhadra.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: In the context of Hindu Mythology, which of the following options is not among the ten avatars of Lord Vishnu? [SSC MTS 06/08/2019]
solution:
(A) Garud
Ten major Avatars of Lord Vishnu (Dashavatara) are Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, Krishna, Buddha and Kalki. Garud - the mount (Vahana) of Lord Vishnu and also considered as the king of birds.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which of the following is associated to the Sanskrit Mahabharata? [SSC MTS 13/08/2019]
solution:
(C) Shanti Parva
Shanti Parva is the 12th book out of the 18 books of epic Mahabharata, written by Sage Vyasa. Gita Govinda was composed by Jayadeva. Yuddha Kand is a chapter of epic Ramayana composed by Valmiki. Kathasaritsagara is a collection of folk tales in Sanskrit by Somadeva.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Panchavati, a key part of the Valmiki Ramayana, is located in which state of India? [SSC CHSL 02/07/2019]
solution:
(C) Maharashtra
Panchvati (Nashik, Maharashtra) is the place where Lord Ram spent a crucial period during exile. The entire Aranya Kanda of Ramayana is set in Panchvati. Some other places of Ramayana are Janakpur (Nepal), Ayodhya (UP), Chitrakoot (MP), Rameshwaram (Tamil Nadu), Kishkindha (Karnataka), Lepakshi (Andhra Pradesh).

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The entrance gateway in Dravidian style temple is known as: [SSC CGL 11/12/2024]
solution:
(A) Gopuram
In Dravidian style temple, Gopuram is the entrance gateway at the front. Mandap is a pillared hall for the public rituals. Vimana is the main temple tower, a pyramidal structure that rises above the sanctum sanctorum (garbhagriha). Shikhar is the rising tower above the garbhagriha in Nagara or North Indian style of architecture.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Guru ________ is known as the cobbler saint. [SSC CPO 16/03/2019]
solution:
(A) Ravidas
Guru Ravidas a 15th century saint from Varanasi. He was known as 'Cobbler Saint' because of his caste. Hymns of Ravidas were included in Guru Granth Sahib. Guru Narhari das was the guru of Tulsidas. Jiva Goswami was a saint from Gaudiya Vaishnav school, known as one of the Six Goswamis of Vrindavan. Ayya Vaikundar also known as Vaikunda Swami was the founder of Ayyavazhi faith.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Nataraja represents the dancing figure of which Hindu deity? [SSC CHSL TIER-II 18/11/2024]
solution:
(A) Shiva
The Nataraja statue represents the dancing form of Lord Shiva. It depicts Shiva performing the cosmic dance 'Tandav', symbolising creation, preservation and destruction of the universe. Nataraja Statue was crafted using the lost-wax technique of Cholas.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Consider the following statements: [CAPF 2021]
1. The early Sikh Gurus were religious preachers and did not interfere in politics but Guru Arjan extended his good wishes for the rebel prince Khusrau.
2. Guru Arjan attempted to organize the finances by introducing the system of more or less compulsory 'spiritual tribute' to be collected by his agents called Masands.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
solution:
(C) Both 1 and 2
Guru Arjan Dev (5th Sikh guru) involved himself in politics and extended his support to Khusrau, the rebellious son of Jahangir. Due to which Jahangir killed Guru Arjan Dev in 1606. He introduced the system of spiritual tribute known as 'Masands'. These Masands were agents of Guru Arjan to collect compulsory tribute, which helped in organizing the finances of the Sikh community.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following Bhakti saints did not belong to Nirguna School of Bhakti? [CAPF 2021]
solution:
(D) Chaitanya
Nirguna bhakti school believes in formless God whereas Sagun bhakti believes in idol worship. Kabir (1440 - 1510): disciple of Ramananda. Belonged to Nirguna sect. Preachings compilation - Bijaka. Guru Nanak (1469 - 1538): founder of Sikhism, belonged to Nirguna school of Bhakti. He belonged to Khatri community. Dadu Dayal (1544 - 1603): belonged to Nirguna sect and founder of Dadu Panth. Chaitanya (1486 - 1533): propounded Bhakti movement in Bengal. King of Gaudiya. He belonged to Sagun school of Bhakti and was a devotee of Lord Krishna.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: What is Nalayira Divyaprabandham? [CAPF 2024]

options.a: Compositions by the Alvars compiled in the form of an anthology
options.d: An exposition of the hymns of Rig Veda in Malayalam

solution:
(A) Compositions by the Alvars compiled in the form of an anthology
Nalayira Divyaprabandham is a collection of 4,000 verses composed by various Alvars in Tamil language. Alvars were the Tamil poet-saints who were devoted to Lord Vishnu. It consists of 12 books written by 12 Alvars. Andal was the first female Alvar.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: What are bhuni, pachara, and dopati mentioned frequently in the biographies of the Vaishnava reformers of medieval Assam? [CAPF 2024]

options.a: Vessels of metal used in religious oblation
options.c: Musical instruments used at the time of Bohag Bihu festival

solution:
(D) Pieces of men's clothes
Bhuni is a simple, modest, unstitched garment, worn by followers of the Vaishnav faith. Pachara is a long cloth wrapped around the waist, often worn in daily life and ceremonies. Dopati is a more formal, ceremonial garment, often worn for religious rituals and significant cultural events.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which one among the following is a temple of the Vaishnavite tradition? [CDS II 2021]
solution:
(A) Srirangam
Sri Ranganathaswamy (Srirangam) is a temple dedicated to Lord Ranganatha, a form of Lord Vishnu. It is located in Tamil Nadu. It belongs to Vaishnavite tradition. Chidambaram temple is famous for Nataraja statue. Temple at Gangaikonda Cholapuram was built by Chola king Rajendra I. Brihadeshwar Temple (Thanjavur) also known as 'Big Temple', built by Rajaraja Chola I. All three temples are dedicated to Lord Shiva, belong to Shaivite tradition and are located in Tamil Nadu.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The biography of Shaikh Muinuddin Chishti, authored by Jahanara, is known as [CDS II 2021]
solution:
(A) Munis al Arwah
Jahanara Begum was the daughter of Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan. She authored 'Munis al-Arwah', the biography of Shaikh Moinuddin Chishti, his dargah is located in Ajmer. Fawaid ul Fawad is a collection of teachings of Khwaja Nizamuddin Auliya, compiled by his disciple, Amir Hasan Ala Sijzi.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The town of Chisht, from which the Sufi Chishti Silsila derives its name, is located in [CDS II 2021]
solution:
(B) Central Afghanistan
The town of Chisht from which the Chishti Silsila of Sufism derives its name is situated in Herat city of Central Afghanistan. The Chishti order was founded in 10th century by Abu Ishaq Shami. Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti introduced Chishti order in India.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The rules for congregational worship (Sangat) involving collective recitation were organized by [CDS II 2021]
solution:
(A) Guru Nanak
Guru Nanak Dev (1469 - 1539): founded Sikh faith. Born in Talwandi (Nankana Sahib, Pakistan) and died in Kartarpur (Pakistan). Started the institution of Guru ka Langar. He was the contemporary of Babur. He established the concept of Sangat, which refers to the Sikh congregation and the rules for Sangat worship, including collective recitation of hymns (shabad).

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was the founder of the Virashaiva Movement in Karnataka? [CDS I 2022]
solution:
(A) Basavanna
Basavanna was the founder of the Virashaiva Movement in Karnataka. It is also known as Lingayat movement. This movement was a reform movement within Hinduism in 12th century. Appar and Sambandar in 7th century and Sundarar in 9th century were Tamil poets. These three were among the 63 Nayanars, who were considered saints in the Shaiva tradition of Tamil Nadu.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which one of the following pairs denoting various forms of 'Bhakti' is not correctly matched? [CDS I 2023]

options.a: Saguna: Belief in Gods with attributes
options.b: Nirguna: Belief in Gods without attributes
options.c: Alvars: Believed in devotion of Shakti
options.d: Nayanars: Believed in devotion of Shiva

solution:
(C) Alvars: Believed in devotion of Shakti
Saguna - poets or saints who believed in Gods with attributes. Nirguna - who believed in Gods without attributes. Alvars - saints or poets who were devoted to Lord Vishnu. Nayanars - saints devoted to Lord Shiva. Shaktas were those who believed in devotion of Shakti.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following is not a woman Nayanar saint? [CDS II 2023]
solution:
(C) Andal
Nayanars were a group of 63 Tamil saints dedicated to the worship of Lord Shiva. The Nayanar's hymns were collected into an anthology called the Tevaram. There were 3 women Nayanars - Karaikkal Ammaiyar, Mangayarkkarasiyar and Isaignaniyar. Andal was the only female Alvar among the 12 Alvars of Vaishnava tradition in South India dedicated to Lord Vishnu.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Which of the following statements about the Vaishnava saint of Assam, Shankardeva, is/are correct? [CDS II 2023]
1. His teachings are often known as the Bhagavati dharma.
2. He encouraged the establishment of satras or monasteries and namghars or prayer halls for the transmission of spiritual knowledge.
3. His major compositions include the Kirtana-ghosha.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
solution:
(C) 1, 2 and 3
Srimanta Sankardeva (1449 - 1568): was a Vaishnavite saint and founder of bhakti movement in Assam. His teachings are often known as the Bhagavati Dharma. He encouraged the establishment of satras (monasteries) and namghars (prayer halls). He is composer of song of Sattriya dance performance. Kirtan-Ghosha was written by Sankardeva.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
How many of the following statements about Bhakti poet Namdev is/are correct? [CDS I 2024]
1. He seems to have played a part in transmitting the southern Bhakti to northern India.
2. He was a rigorous monotheist and opposed caste distinctions.
3. He was a devout follower of Kabir.
Select the correct answer.
solution:
(B) 2
Namdev (1270 - 1350) was the follower of Varkari tradition, a bhakti movement of Maharashtra. Wrote commentary on Bhagavad Gita - Bhavarthadipka (also known as Gnaneswari). Yes, so Namdev was follower of Varkari tradition, and monotheist, opposed caste distinctions. He was not a follower of Kabir. Namdev's period was 1270 to 1350 whereas Kabir's period was 1440 - 1510. Hence statements 1 and 2 are correct, statement 3 is incorrect. Number of correct statements is 2.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which one of the following statements about the Bhakti movement is NOT correct? [NDA II 2023]

options.a: The Bhakti movement avoided traditions that promoted diversity.
options.b: In northern India, the Bhakti movement revolved around the worship of Rama and Krishna.
options.c: The Bhakti movement relied on the devotion of God, both with and without attributes.
options.d: While Tulsidas represented the tradition of devotion with attributes, Sant Kabir followed the path of devotion without attributes.

solution:
(A) The Bhakti movement avoided traditions that promoted diversity.
The Bhakti movement was based on the doctrine that the relationship between man and God is through love and worship and not based on rituals. It encouraged diversity and welcomed various forms of devotions. In northern India, poets like Tulsidas focused on devotion to Ram and Mirabai emphasized Krishna worship. It relied on the devotion of God with attributes or forms by saints like Tulsidas and also with God without attributes like Sant Kabir.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who propounded Kashmir Shaivism? [CDS I 2024]
solution:
(A) Vasugupta
Kashmir Shaivism (Trika) is a non-dual (Advaita) tradition of Shaiva philosophy that developed in Kashmir around 850 AD. Vasugupta was the author of Shiva Sutras and founder of Kashmir Shaivism. Abhinavagupta was the most prominent philosopher of Kashmir Shaivism and wrote Tantraloka.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Consider the following statements about the Vedanta philosophy: [CDS II 2024]
1. Shankara was the founder of Advaita Vedanta.
2. The philosophy of Dvaita was elaborated by Madhva.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
solution:
(C) Both 1 and 2
Adi Shankaracharya was the founder of Advaita Vedanta (non-dualism) school of philosophy. Madhavacharya - Dvaita. Ramanuja - gave the concept of Vishishtadvaita. Nibarkacharya - Dvaitadvaita. Vallabhacharya - Shuddhadvaita.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which one of the following was not a Shaiva tantric sect? [CISF AC 2022]
solution:
(A) Sahajiyas
Kapalikas, a Shaiva tantric sect known for their extreme practices including rituals involving skulls and cremation grounds. Kalamukhas, a Shaiva tantrik sect known for their asceticism. Nathas, Shaiva sect linked with Hath Yoga and Gorakhnath, worship Shiva as supreme Guru and referred him as Adinatha. Sahajiyas refers to a tantric sect of Vaishnavism. They focused on worship of Radha-Krishna.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: The Dakshinamurti (South-facing) form of Shiva represents the [CISF AC 2024]
solution:
(C) universal teacher
The Dakshinamurti form of Shiva represents the Guru form and Divine Teacher. It shows Shiva as a teacher of knowledge, yoga and enlightenment in the context of yoga, music and wisdom.

subject: Medieval History
question_text:
Which of the following statements about Saguna bhakti traditions is/are correct? [NDA I 2021]
1. Saguna bhakti traditions focus on the worship of specific deities such as Vishnu or his avatars.
2. In Saguna bhakti traditions, Gods and Goddesses are conceptualised in anthropomorphic forms.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
solution:
(C) Both 1 and 2
Saguna bhakti traditions focus on the worship of specific deities such as Vishnu and his avatars or Shiva. In these traditions, Gods and Goddesses are conceptualised in anthropomorphic forms, that is, they are visualised and depicted in human like forms.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Who among the following was considered to be the preceptor of Mirabai? [NDA I 2021]
solution:
(B) Raidas
Mirabai (1498 - 1546): disciple of Vallabhacharya. She was Rajput princess and daughter-in-law of Rana Sanga of Mewar, she was married in 1516 to Bhoj Raj, the crown prince of Mewar. She loved Lord Krishna. Sant Ravidas (Raidas), cobbler saint was the guru or preceptor of Mirabai. She is known as - Radha of Rajasthan.

subject: Medieval History
question_text: Which one of the following terms is associated with the practice of Sufism? [NDA I 2025]
solution:
(D) Murid
Terms associated with Sufism in India: Tasawwuf - Sufism, Sheikh/Pir/Murshid - spiritual teacher, Murid - Disciple, Khalifah - successor, Khanqah - the hospice (residential place), Sama - musical recital, Raksa - Dance, Fana - Self Annihilation, Ziyarat - pilgrimage to the tomb of Sufi Saints, Wali - saint or a friend of God.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following was the first Portuguese viceroy of India? [SSC CPO 23/11/2020]
solution:
(C) Francisco de Almeida
Francisco de Almeida was the first Portuguese viceroy of India serving from 1505 to 1509 AD. He was appointed viceroy by King Manuel I of Portugal. He brought Blue Water Policy (Cartaz system). Cartaz system was a licensing system that allowed the Portuguese to control trade in Indian Ocean. He was the first Portuguese to arrive in Bombay by ship in 1509.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following was a Portuguese writer who wrote about the trade and society in South India? [SSC CHSL 10/08/2021]
solution:
(D) Duarte Barbosa
Duarte Barbosa was a Portuguese writer and officer in India between 1500 and 1516. He wrote the 'Book of Duarte Barbosa' (Livro de Duarte Barbosa) in 1516 about trade and society in South India. He visited the court of Krishna Deva Raya.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Vasco da Gama, who discovered the sea route from Europe to India, belonged to which nation? [SSC CPO 27/06/2024]
solution:
(D) Portugal
Vasco Da Gama was the Portuguese explorer, who discovered sea route to India in 1498 AD. He was sent by King Manuel I of Portugal. He came to Calicut for the first time and was welcomed by Zamorin, then ruler of Calicut.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Joseph Francois Dupleix was the Governor General of which of the following East India companies in India? [SSC CHSL 08/08/2023]
solution:
(B) French
Joseph Francois Dupleix was the Governor General of French East India Company from 1742 to 1754. He played a major role in first and second Carnatic wars against British. Dupleix was the one who initially introduced the subsidiary alliance.

subject: Modern History
question_text: The Battle of Bedara was fought between the British Army and the Dutch Army in: [SSC CHSL 11/08/2021]
solution:
(B) 1759
Battle of Bedara also known as Battle of Chinsurah or the Battle of Hooghly, fought on the banks of Hooghly in 1759. Between British East India Company and Dutch East India Company. British Army won this battle and Mir Jafar, then Nawab of Bengal, was replaced by Mir Qasim because he helped the Dutch during the war.

subject: Modern History
question_text: The battle of Plassey was fought between the East India Company, force headed by Robert Clive, and the Nawab of Bengal, ________ in 1757. [SSC PHASE XII 20/06/2024]
solution:
(C) Siraj-Ud-Daulah
Battle of Plassey was fought in 1757 in Bengal. It confirmed the formal beginning of British Empire in India. It was fought between the British East India Company led by Robert Clive and the Nawab of Bengal Siraj-ud-Daulah. British defeated and killed Siraj-Ud-Daulah. Mir Jafar who helped British in this war, became the governor of Bengal.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which city was the capital of the French Colony in India? [SSC MTS 26/10/2021]
solution:
(C) Pondicherry
In 1674 the Governor of French East India Company, Francois Martin, set up trading centre at Pondicherry and it became the chief French settlement in India. It is also called 'India's Little France'. At present, it is a union territory of India and called as Puducherry.

subject: Modern History
question_text: French possessions in India were restored after the Battle of Wandiwash by the Treaty of ________ signed in the year 1763 between Britain and France. [SSC CPO 05/10/2023]
solution:
(B) Paris
Battle of Wandiwash (1760) was the decisive battle during the third Carnatic War (1756 - 1763). It was fought between British forces led by Eyre Coote and French forces led by Comte de Lally. It was won by Britishers who captured Pondicherry. Treaty of Paris (1763): It ended the Seven Year's War between Britain and France and the third Carnatic War. It restored some French possessions in India.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which of the following was the first major and decisive victory for the East India Company on the soil of India? [SSC MTS 13/09/2023]
solution:
(C) Battle of Plassey
Battle of Sindhuli was fought in 1767, this battle was the part of the unification of Nepal led by Prithvi Narayan Shah, King of Gorkha (later King of Nepal) against British forces. The British were defeated. Battle of Tiruvannamalai was fought in 1767 between Hyder Ali Mysore and British East India Company. Hyder Ali was defeated.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following foreign travellers is NOT correctly matched with their respective country they hail from? [SSC CHSL 12/08/2021]
solution:
(B) Peter Mundy - China
Marco Polo (1254 - 1323) was from Italy, Peter Mundy (1600 - 1667) came from England, Ibn Battuta (1304 - 1368) was a Moroccan traveller, Al-Biruni (973 - 1048) was from Khwarizm (Uzbekistan).

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who became the Nawab of Bengal after the death of Alivardi Khan in the year 1756? [SSC MTS 12/11/2024]
solution:
(C) Siraj-Ud-Daulah
Murshid Quli Khan was the first Nawab of Bengal succeeded by Alivardi Khan in 1740. After the death of Alivardi Khan in 1756, Siraj-Ud-Daulah became the Nawab of Bengal at the age of 23 years. He was the Nawab during the Black hole tragedy (1756) of Calcutta at Fort William which led to Battle of Plassey in 1757.

subject: Modern History
question_text: After the decisive battle of Plassey of 1757, who among the following was declared as the Nawab of Bengal by the British? [SSC MTS 04/11/2024]
solution:
(B) Mir Jafar Ali Khan
Mir Jafar was the commander of Siraj-Ud-Daulah. During the battle of Plassey in 1757, Jafar was bribed by Robert Clive and was offered Nawab's post for betraying Siraj-Ud-Daulah, the then Nawab of Bengal.

subject: Modern History
question_text:
Which of the given statements is correct? [SSC MTS 07/07/2022]
I. When Alivardi Khan died in 1756, Siraj-ud-daulah became the nawab of Bengal.
II. In 1757, Robert Clive led the company's army against Siraj-ud-daulah at Plassey.
solution:
(D) Both I and II
After the death of Alivardi Khan in 1756, Siraj-ud-Daulah became the nawab of Bengal. In 1757, British forces were led by Robert Clive against Siraj-ud-Daulah, Nawab of Bengal in Battle of Plassey.

subject: Modern History
question_text: In which year was the Treaty of Amritsar signed? [SSC GD 20/02/2024]
solution:
(A) 1809
The treaty of Amritsar was signed in 1809. Between Charles T. Metcalfe, representing British East India Company and Maharaja Ranjit Singh, leader of Sikh Empire. It established Sutlej River as the boundary between British and Sikhs. Maharaja Ranjit Singh is from Sukerchakiya misl of the 12 misls.

subject: Modern History
question_text: In 1850, Kashmir was sold under which of the following treaties to the Dogra dynasty of Jammu? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 13/10/2023]
solution:
(C) Treaty of Amritsar
Treaty of Amritsar (1846) was signed between British East India Company and Maharaja Gulab Singh of Jammu from Dogra dynasty. The British sold Kashmir to Gulab Singh for a sum of 7.5 million rupees. It was signed after the first Anglo-Sikh War and the Treaty of Lahore in 1845.

subject: Modern History
question_text: The seven years of war in the 18th century was fought between Britain and ________. [SSC CPO 05/10/2023]
solution:
(C) France
The Seven Years War was fought from 1756 to 1763. It was fought between Britain and France with their allies. It was the global conflict fought in Europe, North America, India, Carribean and Philippines. It was also known as French and Indian War.

subject: Modern History
question_text: The second Anglo-Mysore War came to an end by signing which of the following treaties? [SSC CHSL 04/08/2023]
solution:
(D) The Treaty of Mangalore
The Second Anglo-Mysore war was fought during 1780 - 1784, between the British and the Mysore Kingdom. Haider Ali died of cancer and his son Tipu Sultan succeeded him in 1782. He continued Second Anglo-Mysore War till 1784 and signed the treaty of Mangalore to end this war. Warren Hastings was Governor General of Bengal during this war.

subject: Modern History
question_text: The Fourth Anglo-Mysore War was fought in the year ________. [SSC MTS 11/11/2024]
solution:
(D) 1799
The Fourth Anglo-Mysore War was fought in 1799, between Tipu Sultan and British led by Lord Wellesley (Governor General of Bengal). Tipu Sultan was killed and a puppet ruler of Wadiyar dynasty (Mysore) signed the Subsidiary Alliance in 1799. Hyderabad was the first to sign subsidiary alliance in 1798. Subsidiary Alliance was brought by Dupleix (French General) and was successfully implemented by Wellesley.

subject: Modern History
question_text: The British annexed Punjab after defeating the Sikhs in the Anglo-Sikh War in the year ________. [SSC CPO 24/11/2020]
solution:
(C) 1849
During the second Anglo-Sikh war (1848-49), Dalhousie annexed Punjab and john Lawrence became the first chief commissioner of Punjab.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who was the Governor General of Bengal during the 3rd Anglo-Mysore war? [SSC CGL 06/03/2020]
solution:
(B) 2nd Earl Cornwallis
Third Anglo-Mysore War (1790-92) was fought between Tipu Sultan and Britishers. Tipu was defeated and treaty of Seringapatnam was signed. 2nd Earl Cornwallis was the Governor-General of Bengal from 1786 to 1793.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Mir Qasim name is associated with which of the following battles of India? [SSC CHSL 03/08/2023]
solution:
(B) Battle of Buxar
Battle of Buxar (1764) was fought between British East India Company led by Hector Munro and the combined army of Mir Qasim (Nawab of Bengal), Shuja-ud-Daula (Nawab of Awadh) and Shah Alam II (Mughal Emperor). The British won the battle and replaced Mir Qasim with Mir Jafar as Nawab of Bengal. Made Shah Alam II and Shuja-ud-Daula sign Allahabad treaty in 1765 with Governor-General Robert Clive. This treaty separately divided Diwani functions (given to Britishers) and Nizamat functions (given to Shah Alam II).

subject: Modern History
question_text: The Battle of Karnal is known for the invasion of which of the following aggressors against India? [SSC CHSL 14/08/2023]
solution:
(A) Nadir Shah
The Battle of Karnal was fought in 1739, it was a decisive victory of Nadir Shah of Iran against the Mughal ruler Muhammad Shah. Nadir Shah was the founder of Afsharid dynasty. He captured Delhi and took Peacock throne (built by Shah Jahan) and Kohinoor diamond from Mughal treasure.

subject: Modern History
question_text: In which of these battles the involvement of the Mughal army was not there? [SSC CPO 10/11/2022]
solution:
(D) Battle of Aliwal
Battle of Aliwal (1846) was fought between the British, led by Harry Smith against the Sikhs, led by Ranjodh Singh Majithia. It was part of 1st Anglo-Sikh war and won by the British.

subject: Modern History
question_text: In the 18th century, the British fought ________ wars with the Mysore rulers. [SSC MTS 07/07/2022]
solution:
(D) Four
In 18th century, the British and Mysore rulers fought 4 wars known as Anglo-Mysore wars. 1st Anglo-Mysore War (1767-69): Mysore kingdom won. Treaty of Madras signed. 2nd Anglo-Mysore War (1780-84): Tipu Sultan sat on throne. War ended with Treaty of Mangalore. 3rd Anglo-Mysore War (1790-92): defeat of Tipu Sultan. Treaty of Seringapatnam signed. 4th Anglo-Mysore War (1798-99): Tipu Sultan was killed and Subsidiary Alliance was signed with Wodeyar dynasty in 1799.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Tipu Sultan became the ruler of Mysore in ________. [SSC MTS 14/07/2022 + GD 22/02/2024]
solution:
(B) 1782
Haider Ali died of cancer in 1782, during the Second Anglo Mysore War. He was succeeded by his son Tipu Sultan (1782 - 1799), who continued the war and signed the Treaty of Mangalore in 1784 with the British to end the war.

subject: Modern History
question_text: In which year did Tipu Sultan die defending his capital Seringapatam? [SSC MTS 13/07/2022]
solution:
(A) 1799
Tipu Sultan was killed in the fourth Anglo-Mysore War in 1799. The British forces allied with Nizam of Hyderabad and Marathas to defeat him. The British crowned Krishnaraja Wodeyar III as the King of Mysore and signed Subsidiary Alliance with him in 1799.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who emerged victorious in 'The Siege of Arcot' in 1751 AD? [SSC MTS 05/10/2021]
solution:
(D) British
The British East India Company, led by Robert Clive emerged victorious in the Siege of Arcot in 1751. The British successfully defeated the combined forces of Nawab of Carnatic, Chanda Sahib and French East India Company.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which of the following battles did Maulvi Ahmadullah Shah fight, defeating the forces under Henry Lawrence? [SSC CHSL 04/08/2021]
solution:
(C) Battle of Chinhat
Maulvi Ahmadullah Shah fought and defeated the British forces under Henry Lawrence in Battle of Chinhat of 1857. He was the commander of army of rebels under Barkat Ahmad. It was fought near Chinhat village, Lucknow (Awadh Province).

subject: Modern History
question_text: In the Battle of Swally (1612) the British fought against the ________. [SSC CHSL 06/08/2021]
solution:
(B) Portuguese
The Battle of Swally (Suvali) was fought in 1612. It was a naval battle near Surat between the British and the Portuguese. The British forces led by Thomas Best defeated the Portuguese. It was the first battle fought by British in India.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who signed the treaty of Alinagar with the British? [SSC CGL 09/03/2020]
solution:
(B) Siraj-ud-Daula
Treaty of Alinagar was signed in 1757 by Robert Clive from British East India Company and Siraj-ud-Daula, Nawab of Bengal. Under this treaty the Nawab restored Calcutta to the British and accepted the provisions of Farrukhsiyar's farman of 1717, that the British goods which passed through Bengal would be tax free.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Europeans entered India with a mindset of trading only. This streak was initiated by which of the following European nations? [SSC CPO 04/10/2023]
solution:
(D) Portuguese
Portuguese were the first European nation to enter India with the primary aim of trade. Vasco da Gama (Portuguese) arrived Calicut (Kozhikode, Kerala) in 1498 and was the first to discover the sea route to India. In India, the Dutch arrived in 1605, the British arrived in 1608 and the French arrived in 1664.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Chandannagar was established as a French colony in 1673, obtaining permission from ________ the then Nawab of Bengal, to establish a trading post on the right bank of the river Hooghly. [SSC CGL 18/04/2022]
solution:
(C) Ibrahim Khan
Chandannagar was established as French colony in 1673, obtaining permission from Ibrahim Khan, Nawab of Bengal. It became a permanent French settlement in 1688. It was governed as a part of French India until 1950s.

subject: Modern History
question_text: The Bara Imambara in Lucknow was built in the year 1784 by ________. [SSC MTS 14/11/2024]
solution:
(B) Nawab Asaf-ud-Daula
The Bara Imambara in Lucknow was built by Asaf-ud-Daula the Nawab of Awadh in 1784. It is the world's second largest after Nizamat Imambara. Nawab Asaf-ud-Daula was the son of Nawab Shuja-ud-Daula.

subject: Modern History
question_text: At which one of the following places did the Danes establish their settlement in India? [NDA I 2023]
solution:
(D) Tranquebar
The Danes (Danish) established settlements in Tranquebar (Tamil Nadu) and Serampore (West Bengal). The Danish East India Company was formed in 1616. In 1620, the Danish signed a treaty allowing them to establish a trading post at Tranquebar.

subject: Modern History
question_text:
Chronologically arrange the following treaties/conventions signed between the Marathas and the British (starting with the earliest): [CDS I 2021]
1. Treaty of Salbai
2. Treaty of Purandar
3. Convention of Wadgaon
4. Treaty of Surat
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
solution:
(A) 4-2-3-1
Treaty of Surat (1775): it was signed between Raghunathrao of Marathas and British East India Company. It led to the first Anglo-Maratha War from 1775 - 1782 AD. Treaty of Purandar (1776): It was signed between Marathas and British. It rejected Treaty of Surat. Convention of Wadgaon (1779): It was a result of Maratha's victory over the British in the Battle of Talegaon / Wadgaon. Treaty of Salbai (1782): ended the first Anglo-Maratha War. Treaty between Marathas and British. It recognised Madhavrao II as the Peshwa.

subject: Modern History
question_text: The Dutch were defeated by Marthanda Varma of Travancore Kingdom in the battle of: [CDS II 2022]
solution:
(D) Colachel
Marthanda Varma of Travancore (Kerala) adopted an expansionist strategy. Marthanda Varma defeated the Dutch in the Battle of Colachel (Kulachal) in 1741 and reduced the Dutch influence in India.

subject: Modern History
question_text: The Treaty of Yandabo was concluded as part of which one of the following wars? [NDA I 2023]
solution:
(A) First Anglo - Burmese War
Treaty of Yandabo (1826): It was the peace treaty that ended the first Anglo - Burmese War that broke out on 5 March 1824. It was signed by British General Archibald Campbell and Burmese Governor of Legaing Maha Min Hla Kyaw Htin.

subject: Modern History
question_text: In the context of eighteenth century India, what was 'Dastak'? [CDS I 2023]
solution:
(C) Trade Permit
Dastak was a trade permit, essentially a pass or permission, issued by the East India Company to its employees, allowing them to trade duty-free, especially in Bengal.

subject: Modern History
question_text:
With reference to Indian history, consider the following statements: [IAS 2022]
1. The Dutch established their factories/warehouses on the east coast on lands granted to them by Gajapati rulers.
2. Alfonso de Albuquerque captured Goa from the Bijapur Sultanate.
3. The English East India Company established a factory at Madras on a plot of land leased from a representative of the Vijayanagara empire.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
solution:
(B) 2 and 3 only
Rule of Gajapati dynasty ended in 1542. The Dutch East India company was formed in 1602, and established their first factory at Masulipatnam (Andhra Pradesh) in 1605. Alfonso de Albuquerque (Portuguese) captured Goa from Bijapur Sultanate in 1510. The English East India Company established a factory at Madras by leasing land from a local Nayaka, a representative of Vijayanagar empire.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following rulers of medieval Gujarat surrendered Diu to Portuguese? [IAS 2023]
solution:
(C) Bahadur Shah
Bahadur Shah (1525 - 1537) was the ruler of Gujarat Sultanate. In 1534, Bahadur Shah signed the Treaty of Bassein with the Portuguese in which he ceded Diu to the Portuguese with some other territories. The Portuguese obtained Daman from Bahadur Shah in 1559.

subject: Modern History
question_text:
Which of the following statements is/are correct? [NDA II 2022]
1. The Dutch East India Company was formed after the formation of the British East India Company.
2. Vasco da Gama reached Calicut in 1498.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
solution:
(C) Both 1 and 2
The Dutch East India Company was formed in 1602 whereas the British East India Company was formed with the Royal Charter by Queen Elizabeth I in 1600. Vasco Da Gama arrived in India on 20 May 1498, led by Gujarati pilot named Abdul Majid, at Calicut in the reign of Hindu ruler of Calicut, the Zamorin (Samuthiri).

subject: Modern History
question_text: Where in South India did the British East India Company construct a trading post in 1639? [NDA II 2022]
solution:
(B) Madraspatnam
In 1639, the British East India Company established a trading post and built Fort St. George in Madraspatnam (Madras) on the land taken on lease from Damarla Venkatadri Nayaka, representative of Vijayanagar Empire.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which one of the following battles could be seen as laying the formal foundation of the British Raj in India? [NDA II 2022]
solution:
(B) Battle of Plassey
Battle of Plassey (1757): it laid the formal foundation of British Raj in India. It was fought between British East India Company led by Robert Clive and the Nawab of Bengal Siraj-ud-Daulah. The British forces won and installed Mir Jafar as their puppet Nawab of Bengal.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which one of the following areas was acquired by the British under the Treaty of Deogaon, 1803? [NDA II 2022]
solution:
(A) Cuttack
Treaty of Deogaon (1803): it was signed between the British East India Company and the Bhonsle dynasty of Marathas. The Bhonsles ceded the territories of Cuttack, Balasore to the British. Treaty of Rajghat was signed by Holkars with British in 1805, in which they gave up Tonk, Bundi and Rampura to British.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Maize (makka) was introduced into India in the seventeenth century via: [NDA I 2025]
solution:
(A) Portugal
Maize also known as corn or Makka is a native crop to Central America. It was introduced in India during the 17th century by the Portuguese and Italian travellers. Portuguese also brought chillis, tomatoes, cashews, tobacco, pineapple, papayas, etc. Vasco Da Gama was the first Portuguese to visit India in 1498 at Calicut.

subject: Modern History
question_text: At which one among the following places did the British East India Company found its factory in the year 1611? [NDA I 2025]
solution:
(B) Masulipatnam
British East India Company was formed in 1599 AD. In 1600, they received royal charter from Queen Elizabeth I to trade in India. In 1608, William Hawkings visited Jahangir's court to seek permission to trade in India. In 1611, the British established their 1st temporary factory at Masaulipatnam (Andhra Pradesh) and in 1613, they established their first permanent factory at Surat.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who of the following social reformers of 19th century India was author of Stri Purush Tulana? [SSC CGL 25/08/2024]
solution:
(B) Tarabai Shinde
Stree Purush Tulana was a comparison between men and women, it was published in 1882 in Marathi. It was written by Tarabai Shinde. She was a member of Satyashodhak Samaj and associate of Savitribai Phule and Jyotirao Phule.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who founded the Prarthana Samaj in Mumbai in 1867? [SSC CGL 23/08/2024]
solution:
(D) Atmaram Pandurang
Prarthna Samaj or Prarthana Mandali in Mumbai (then Bombay) was founded by Atmaram Pandurang in 1867. Its aim was to make people believe in one God. It was joined by Mahadev Govind Ranade in 1870.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which Governor General of British India helped Raja Ram Mohan Roy legally abolish the sati practice? [SSC CGL 23/08/2024]
solution:
(D) Lord William Bentinck
Lord William Bentick, Governor General of India, legally abolished Sati by passing Bengal Sati Regulation act in 1829, following the efforts of Raja Ram Mohan Roy. He was the first Governor General of India.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who founded the Brahmo Samaj in Calcutta in 1828? [SSC CGL 18/08/2024]
solution:
(A) Ram Mohan Roy
Raja Ram Mohan Roy (1772 - 1833): founded Brahmo Samaj in 1828. Also known as 'Father of Indian Renaissance'. Akbar II gave him the title of 'Raja'. Founded Atmiya Sabha in 1814. He converted Vedas in Bengali and was against idol worship.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which of the following Sikh reform movements/terms was started in 1873 in Amritsar? [SSC CGL 17/08/2024]
solution:
(B) Singh Sabha Movement
The Singh Sabha Movement was started in 1873 in Amritsar. It was started with the formation of Sanatan Singh Sabha in Amritsar in 1873 followed by Tat Khalsa Singh Sabha in Lahore in 1879. It was a movement to eliminate other religions practices within Sikhism.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following founded the Atmiya Sabha in 1815? [SSC CGL 17/08/2024]
solution:
(D) Raja Rammohan Roy
Raja Ram Mohan Roy founded the Atmiya Sabha in 1814 in Calcutta. It was the forerunner to the Brahmo Samaj. It aimed to propagate the monotheistic ideals of Vedanta and to campaign against idolatry, caste, meaningless rituals, etc.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who founded the Arya Samaj in Mumbai in 1875? [SSC CGL 24/07/2024]
solution:
(B) Swami Dayanand Saraswati
Swami Dayanand Saraswati founded Arya Samaj in Bombay in 1875. His original name was 'Mool Shankar'. Opened first unit of Arya Samaj in Bombay and later in Lahore. Advocated for caste less and class less society. He gave slogans, Back to Vedas and India for Indians. Wrote book 'Satyarth Prakash'. Believed in Karma and reincarnation.

subject: Modern History
question_text: In which year was the temple entry movement started by Bhimrao Ambedkar? [SSC CGL 24/07/2024]
solution:
(B) 1927
The Temple Entry movement in North India (1927) was started by BR Ambedkar, he started Mahad Satyagraha (1927) to enter the Veerbhadra Temple in Maharashtra and burnt Manusmriti. To grant Dalits access to temples. Temple entry Movement started in South India in 1924 by T.K. Madhavan. Vaikom Satyagraha by K.P. Keshava in 1924.

subject: Modern History
question_text:
With reference to Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar, consider the following statements: [SSC CGL 24/07/2024]
I. He protested child marriage and polygamy.
II. Due to his efforts, the first Hindu Widow Remarriage Act, 1856 was introduced.
III. He was primarily known for his scholarship in Persian language.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
solution:
(A) I, II
Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar was a scholar in Sanskrit language. He was the Principal of Sanskrit college. In 1856, Hindu Widow Remarriage Act was passed by his efforts. He was against Child marriage and also opposed Polygamy. He wrote Bidhobabivah (1855) on widow remarriage and Bahubivah (1871) against Polygamy.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who suggested the law that the British Government passed in 1856 to support widow remarriage? [SSC CGL 25/08/2024]
solution:
(D) Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar
Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar, the principal of Sanskrit college of Calcutta. He was credited for widow remarriage act of 1856. This law was drafted by Lord Dalhousie and was passed into law by Lord Canning.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who founded the Theosophical Society in New York in 1875? [SSC CGL 25/08/2024]

options.a: Baba Dayal Das and Madan Lal
options.d: Madame HP Blavatsky and Colonel Olcott

solution:
(D) Madame HP Blavatsky and Colonel Olcott
Theosophical Society was founded by Madame Helena Petrovna Blavatsky (Russia), Henry Steel Olcott (America) in New York in 1875. It was influenced by Hindu belief system and introduced eastern philosophies like Hinduism and Buddhism to the West. Annie Besant also took its membership in 1889 and became its president in 1907.

subject: Modern History
question_text:
Match the following social reform organizations with their respective founders: [SSC CGL 26/08/2024]
Social Reform Organizations - Their Founder
a. Brahmo Samaj - i. Swami Vivekananda
b. Ramakrishna Mission - ii. Atmaram Pandurang
c. Prarthana Samaj - iii. Raja Ram Mohan Roy
d. Arya Samaj - iv. Dayanand Saraswati
Select the correct option:
solution:
(A) a-iii, b-i, c-ii, d-iv
Brahmo Samaj was founded by Raja Ram Mohan Roy in 1828. Ramakrishna Mission was founded by Swami Vivekananda 1897. Prarthana Samaj was founded by Atmaram Pandurang in 1867. Arya Samaj was founded by Swami Dayanand Saraswati in 1875.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Belur Math was founded by which of the following social reformers in British India? [SSC CGL 26/08/2024]
solution:
(A) Swami Vivekanand
Swami Vivekananda, original name was 'Narendranath Dutta'. Birth: 12 Jan 1863, celebrated as Youth Day. Died on 4 July, 1902. He wrote Raja Yoga and Karma Yoga. Believed in idol worship. He was the disciple of Ramakrishna Paramhans (priest of Kali Mandir, Calcutta) and started Ramakrishna Mission in 1897 and made Belur Math (Howrah) its headquarter. In 1893, he gave his famous speech at Chicago conference. His rock memorial is in Kanyakumari (Tamil Nadu).

subject: Modern History
question_text: Guru Ghasidas led which of the following movements to improve the social condition of leather workers in British India? [SSC CGL 26/08/2024]
solution:
(B) Satnami Movement
Satnami Movement is a socio-religious movement founded by Guru Ghasidas of Satnami sect in 1820s in Chhattisgarh region. It was aimed to uplift the social status of leather workers and was against caste discrimination. Satnami sect was founded by Birbhan in 1657.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Where was the first headquarters of the Theosophical Society established in India? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 10/12/2024]
solution:
(A) Adyar
Theosophical Society was established by Madame HP Blavatsky and Colonel Olcott in New York in 1875. In 1882, its headquarters were shifted from New York to Adyar in Madras (Chennai). It was joined by Annie Besant, who became its president in 1907.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following scholars is associated with the Tibet-home theory of the Aryans? [SSC CGL 07/03/2020]
solution:
(A) Dayanand Saraswati
Swami Dayanand Saraswati gave the Tibet-home theory of the Aryans in his book Satyarth Prakash. He proposed that Tibet was the original homeland of the Aryans. Bal Gangadhar Tilak wrote the book 'The Arctic Home in the Vedas' and proposed that Aryans originated in the Arctic Region. Max Muller translated Rigveda in English, he said Aryans belonged to Central Asia.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which of the following statements about Swami Dayanand Saraswati is INCORRECT? [SSC CGL 13/08/2021]

options.b: He authored the book Satyarth Prakash.
options.c: He was the founder of Arya Samaj.
options.d: He was the founder of Brahmo Samaj.

solution:
(D) He was the founder of Brahmo Samaj
Swami Dayanand Saraswati was born in Gujarat in 1824, his original name was 'Mool Shankar'. He founded Arya Samaj in 1875 in Bombay with 10 principles. Authored the book 'Satyarth Prakash' and Veda-Bhashya Bhumika. Brahmo Samaj was founded by Raja Ram Mohan Roy in 1828 in Calcutta.

subject: Modern History
question_text:
Match the following movements with their respective founders: [SSC STENOGRAPHER 27/06/2024]
a. Faraizi Movement - i. Syed Ahmad of Rae Bareli
b. Mohammedan Literary Society - ii. Sir Syed Ahmad Khan
c. Indian Patriotic Association - iii. Nawab Abdul Latif
d. Wahabi Movement - iv. Haji Shariatullah
Select the correct option:
solution:
(C) a-iv, b-iii, c-ii, d-i
Faraizi Movement was founded by Haji Shariatullah in 1818 in Bengal. Mohammedan Literacy Society was founded in 1863 by Nawab Abdul Latif in Calcutta. Indian Patriotic Association or alliance was founded in 1888 by Syed Ahmad Khan and Raja Shiv Prasad Singh of Banaras. Wahabi Movement in India was founded by Sayyid Ahmad Barelvi of Rae Bareli around 1820.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following was the pioneer of widow remarriages in Maharashtra and also started a girls' school in Poona along with his wife? [SSC CPO 27/06/2024]
solution:
(D) Jyotirao Govindrao Phule
Jyotirao Phule along with his wife Savitribai Phule (1st female teacher of India) started the first school for girls in Pune in 1848. He was the pioneer of widow remarriages in Maharashtra and fought against caste discrimination and gender inequality in society.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following wrote Gulamgiri? [SSC CPO 28/06/2024]
solution:
(A) Jyotiba Phule
Jyotiba Phule wrote Gulamgiri (means Slavery) in 1873 and Sarvajanik Satyadharma Pustak in 1889. Both books in Marathi language. In Gulamgiri, he criticised the caste system and compares condition of lower castes in India with other countries.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which amongst the following statements about the Indian social reformers of the 19th century is INCORRECT? [SSC CPO 28/06/2024]

options.a: Mahadev Govind Ranade is associated with the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha
options.b: Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar was the founder of the Brahmo Samaj.
options.c: Dayanand Saraswati founded the Arya Samaj in 1875.
options.d: The Aligarh Movement was started by Sir Syed Ahmad Khan in 1875.

solution:
(B) Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar was the founder of the Brahmo Samaj
Poona Sarvajanik Sabha was founded by GV Joshi and Mahadev Govind Ranade in 1870. Dayanand Saraswati founded Arya Samaj in 1875. Sir Syed Ahmad Khan started Aligarh movement in 1875. Brahmo Samaj was founded by Raja Ram Mohan Roy in 1828, Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar was a member of Brahmo Samaj, he put his efforts to bring widow remarriage act of 1856.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Satyashodhak Samaj (truth seeker society) was established by which of the following social reformers? [SSC CPO 29/06/2024]
solution:
(D) Jyotiba Phule
Satyashodhak Samaj was founded in 1873 by Jyotiba Phule in Pune, Maharashtra against Caste Discrimination, with the leadership of the samaj coming from backward classes. The main aim was social service and spread of education among women and lower caste people.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Sayyid Ahmed Khan founded Mohammedan Anglo-Oriental College in ________ in 1875. [SSC MTS 26/07/2022]
solution:
(A) Aligarh
Mohammedan Anglo-Oriental College in 1875 was founded by Syed Ahmad Khan in Aligarh. It was established as a Science School for the Muslims of India in 1875. It later became the Aligarh Muslim University in 1920.

subject: Modern History
question_text: The Begums of Bhopal founded a primary school for girls in the early twentieth century at ________. [SSC MTS 07/07/2022]
solution:
(C) Aligarh
Between 1819 and 1926, four women ruled the state of Bhopal. Kudsia Begum, Sikander Begum, Shah Jahan Begum and Sultan Jahan Begum. Sultan Jahan Begum (1901 - 1926) founded a primary school for girls in Aligarh in 20th century. She became the first Indian women Chancellor of Aligarh Muslim university in 1920. She founded All India Ladies Association in 1918. Also started Art School in 1905 for widows and destitute women.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Shuddhi movement was initiated by which of the following organisations? [SSC MTS 18/10/2024]
solution:
(C) Arya Samaj
Shuddhi movement was initiated by Arya Samaj and its founder was Swami Dayanand Saraswati. It was started in 1920s for purification of people who converted to Christianity, and to bring them back to Hinduism. It got momentum in 1923 when Swami Shraddhanand established the Bhartiya Hindu Shuddhi Mahasabha.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following was associated with the Young Bengal Movement? [SSC GD 21/02/2024]
solution:
(A) Henry Derozio
Henry Vivian Derozio was an Anglo-Indian poet and teacher of Hindu College in Calcutta. He founded the Young Bengal Movement in 1829. He inspired a generation of Bengali students with his liberal ideas.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who is known as the "Father of the Indian Renaissance" and played a pivotal role in Bengal Renaissance movement? [SSC GD 22/02/2024]
solution:
(A) Raja Ram Mohan Roy
Raja Ram Mohan Roy is also known as the 'Father of Indian Renaissance' and played important role in Bengal Renaissance movement. He wrote Gift to monotheism, Sambad Kaumudi, Precepts of Jesus, Mirat-ul-Akhbar. Rabindranath Tagore wrote Gitanjali and Bankim Chandra Chatterjee wrote Anandmath.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following founded the Vedanta College in 1825? [SSC CHSL 05/08/2022]
solution:
(C) Raja Ram Mohan Roy
Vedanta college in Calcutta was established by Raja Ram Mohan Roy in 1825. It offered courses in both Indian learning and Western sciences. In 1822, Roy founded the Anglo-Hindu School. He wrote Gaudiya Vyakaran book on Bangla Grammar.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which of the following was known as the forerunner of the Brahmo Samaj? [SSC CGL 02/08/2022]
solution:
(B) Atmiya Sabha
Atmiya Sabha was founded by Raja Ram Mohan Roy in 1814 in Calcutta. It is considered as the forerunner of Brahmo Samaj (1828).

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who established India's first girl's school? [SSC GD 29/02/2024]
solution:
(C) Savitribai Phule
Savitribai Phule along with her husband Jyotirao Phule, established India's first school for girls at Bhidewada in Pune, Maharashtra in 1848. Savitribai was an author and have published Kavya Phule and Bavan Kashi Subodh Ratnakar. Also wrote a poem - Go, Get Education.

subject: Modern History
question_text: In 1930, ________ led a satyagraha for the rights of untouchables' entry to the Kalaram temple at Nasik. [SSC CGL 01/08/2022]
solution:
(B) Dr. BR Ambedkar
The Kalaram Temple entry movement was led by Dr. BR Ambedkar in 1930 in Nasik. It is also known as Nasik Satyagraha, aimed to allow Dalits access and entry within the Kalaram Temple.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who founded the Arya Mahila Samaj? [SSC CGL 01/08/2024]
solution:
(C) Pandita Ramabai
The Arya Mahila Samaj was founded by Pandita Ramabai Saraswati in 1882 with the aim of empowering and educating the women. It was founded in Pune.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Dhondo Keshav Karve was an eminent Indian who championed the cause of ________. [SSC CGL 2022, 07/12/2022]
solution:
(D) spread of women education
Dhondo Keshav Karve (DK Karve) championed the cause of spread of women education. Founded first Women's University in India - SNDT University in 1916. Established Maharashtra Girls Education Society in 1896.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Brahmo Samaj was earlier known as: [SSC CHSL 08/07/2020]
solution:
(B) Brahmo Sabha
The Brahmo Sabha was formed by Raja Ram Mohan Roy in 1828, it was a group that believed in monotheism and rejected idol worshiping, caste distinctions. In 1830, it was formally renamed as Brahmo Samaj. Later in 1866, Brahmo Samaj split into two: Brahmo Samaj of India and Adi Brahmo Samaj.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which was the first socio-religious organisation established in Bombay in 1840 to work for the annihilation of caste? [SSC CHSL 10/07/2020]
solution:
(D) Paramhans Mandali
Paramhans Mandali was the first socio-religious organisation established in Bombay in 1849. It was founded by Dadoba Pandurang and Mehtaji Durgaram. It was aimed to break down caste barriers, promote women's education, and advocate for widow remarriage.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which organisation was started in the Pune district of Maharashtra in the 1870s with the aim of promoting education and social rights among the underprivileged groups of the society? [SSC CHSL 11/07/2020]
solution:
(B) Satyashodhak Samaj
Jyotiba Phule established the Satyashodhak Samaj (Truth Seeker's Society) in 1873 in Pune for promoting education and social rights among underprivileged groups of society. Jyotiba Phule also opened a home for widows in 1854.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following social reformers wrote Brahmo Covenant, which was a statement of the creed of the Brahmo Samaj? [SSC MTS 14/10/2024]
solution:
(C) Debendranath Tagore
Brahmo Covenant, also known as Brahmo Samaj Covenant, was a document outlining the duties and obligations of its members. It was written by Debendranath Tagore after joining the Brahmo Samaj in 1842. He established Tattvabodhini Sabha in 1839 to promote Hinduism based on Vedanta and Upanishads. Formed the Adi Brahmo Samaj in 1866. Founded Shantiniketan in 1863.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following formed an organisation to support widow remarriage in Madras Presidency? [SSC CHSL 04/11/2020]
solution:
(A) Veereshlingam Pantulu
Kandukuri Veereshlingam Pantulu established 'Widow Remarriage Association' in 1874 and organised widow remarriages. He published 'Vivekavardhini' journal to promote women's education. He is known as 'Father of Telugu Renaissance Movement'.

subject: Modern History
question_text: In which among the following years was Hindu widow remarriage declared legal in India? [SSC MTS 08/10/2024]
solution:
(C) 1856
Hindu Widow Remarriage Act declared legal in 1856. It was passed by Lord Canning and was drafted by Lord Dalhousie. Act passed with the efforts of Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar. He used verses of Parasara Samiti written by Sage Parashara, which supported widow remarriage.

subject: Modern History
question_text: What was the objective of "Arya Samaj" founded by "Swami Dayanand Saraswati"? [SSC MTS 13/06/2023]

options.c: To encourage the practice of barter trade

solution:
(D) To reform Hinduism
In 1875, Swami Dayanand Saraswati founded Arya Samaj in Bombay, to reform Hinduism. He received education on Vedanta from a blind teacher Swami Virajananda in Mathura. Arya Samaj believed in Chaturvarna system based on occupation and not by birth.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which of following organisations sought to revive Vedic learning and combine it with modern education in the sciences? [SSC MTS 28/10/2024]
solution:
(B) Arya Samaj
Arya Samaj was founded by Swami Dayanand Saraswati in 1875 in Bombay. Headquarter was set up in Lahore. It was against idol worship. Arya Samaj was divided into College Party and Mahatma Party in 1893.

subject: Modern History
question_text: With which of the following organisations was Henry Louis Vivian Derozio, a teacher of Hindu College of Calcutta, associated? [SSC MTS 30/10/2024]
solution:
(D) Young Bengal Movement
Henry Louis Vivian Derozio, was a teacher in Hindu College of Calcutta. He was the founder of Young Bengal Movement in late 1820s. His followers were known as Derozians. Surendranath Banerjee described the Derozians as 'the pioneers of the modern civilisation of Bengal'.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Where was the Hindu college established in the year 1791 to teach ancient Sanskrit texts? [SSC MTS 30/09/2024]
solution:
(C) Banaras
The Hindu College was established in Banaras (Varanasi) in 1791 to teach ancient Sanskrit texts. It was named Sanskrit College and was renamed as Hindu College. It was established by Jonathan Duncan and Governor General Cornwallis for study of Hindu law and philosophy.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following was NOT associated with the 'Prarthana Sabha'? [SSC MTS 16/10/2024]
solution:
(A) Mahatma Gandhi
Prarthana Sabha (Samaj) was a reformist movement founded in 1867 in Bombay. It was founded by Atmaram Pandurang and was joined by M G Ranade in 1870. RG Bhandarkar was a key leader of this samaj. Mahatma Gandhi was not associated with Prarthana Samaj, he arrived in India on 9 Jan 1915.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following founded the Servants of India Society in 1905 with the help of MG Ranade? [SSC MTS 30/10/2024]
solution:
(A) Gopal Krishna Gokhale
Servants of India Society in 1905 was founded by Gopal Krishna Gokhale in Pune with the help of MG Ranade, to train dedicated Indians to serve the country selflessly. Its ideals inspired Mahatma Gandhi, who considered Gokhale as his political Guru.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following was the pioneer of 'Bhoodan Movement'? [SSC MTS 22/10/2024]
solution:
(A) Acharya Vinoba Bhave
The Bhoodan Movement also known as the 'Land Gift Movement' was a non-violent land reform movement started by Acharya Vinoba Bhave in 1951. He was a disciple of Mahatma Gandhi. It was started in Pochampally village of Telangana (then, Hyderabad State). Vinoba Bhave was the first Indian recipient of the Ramon Magsaysay Award, which he received in 1958.

subject: Modern History
question_text: When was the Dev Samaj founded in Lahore by Shiv Narayan Agnihotri? [SSC MTS 13/06/2023]
solution:
(B) 1887
Dev Samaj was founded in 1887 by Shiv Narayan Agnihotri in Lahore. Agnihotri was the member of Brahmo Samaj, he resigned and formed Dev Samaj. It emphasised on the supremacy of 'Guru'. Its teachings were compiled in a book, 'Dev Shastra'.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following social reformers ruled out the practice of Sati on the basis of Hindu religious texts? [SSC MTS 14/11/2024]
solution:
(A) Raja Ram Mohan Roy
Sati was a practice in which a widow burns herself after her husband's death. Raja Ram Mohan Roy led British Governor-General of India William Bentinck to enact the Bengal Sati Regulation act in 1829.

subject: Modern History
question_text:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct about Prarthana Samaj? [SSC CHSL TIER-II 10/01/2024]
I. Prarthana Samaj or 'Prayer Society' in Sanskrit, was a movement for religious and social reforms.
II. Prarthana Samaj was founded in 1863 by Dadoba Pandurang and his brother Atmaram Pandurang.
solution:
(B) Only I
Prarthana Samaj or 'Prayer Society' in Sanskrit, was a movement for religious and social reforms such as caste discrimination, promotion of women's education, widow remarriage etc. it was founded by in 1867, by Atmaram Pandurang in Bombay.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Annie Besant laid the foundation of the Central Hindu College in ________ in 1898 where both Hindu religion and Western scientific subjects were taught. [SSC MTS 18/05/2023]
solution:
(C) Banaras
Annie Besant was a British reformer and Indian independence leader, came to India in 1893. She laid the foundation of the Central Hindu College in Banaras in 1898 where both Hindu religion and Western scientific subjects were taught. The college became the nucleus for the formation of Banaras Hindu University in 1916.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Sree Narayana Guru considered the differences based on caste and religion as meaningless and advocated the principle of 'One Caste, one Religion and one God' for all. He belonged to which of the following organisations? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 12/10/2023]
solution:
(B) Sree Narayana Dharma Paripalana Yogam
Shree Narayana Guru was a social reformer and spiritual leader from Kerala. He advocated for the principal of 'One Caste, one Religion and one god' for all. He was associated with the Sree Narayana Dharma Paripalana (SNDP) Yogam for the upliftment of Ezhavas community of Kerala. It was also known as Aruvippuram Movement (In 1888, Shiv temple was built in Aruvippuram, Kerala).

subject: Modern History
question_text: Chembeti Sridharalu Naidu was a social reformer and was associated with which of the following organisations? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 12/10/2023]
solution:
(A) Veda Samaj
Veda Samaj was a social reform movement started by Keshab Chandra Sen and K. Sridharalu Naidu in Madras in 1864. It was also known as Brahmo Samaj of South India. Chembeti Sridharalu Naidu was its most popular leader, he translated books of the Veda Samaj in Tamil and Telugu.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which of the following is related to a purificatory ceremony called shuddi for those Hindus who had been converted to other religions like Islam and Christianity? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 12/10/2023]
solution:
(D) Arya Samaj
The 'Shuddhi Movement' was started by Arya Samaj for purification of those Hindus who were converted to Islam or Christianity. It was started during 1920s.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which of the following organizations was founded in Poona, Satara and other towns of Maharashtra that worked against Brahmanical orthodoxy and tried to reform Hinduism? [SSC CPO 03/10/2023]
solution:
(B) Paramhansa Mandali
The Paramhansa Mandali was established by Dadoba Pandurang and Durgaram Mehtaji in 1849 in Maharashtra. It fought against idolatry and caste barriers. Branches of the Mandali were formed in Poona, Satara, and other towns of Maharashtra to reform Hinduism.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following was NOT associated with 'Prarthana Sabha'? [SSC CPO 04/10/2023]
solution:
(C) Motilal Nehru
In 1867, Prarthana Samaj (Sabha) was founded by Atmaram Pandurang with the help of Keshab Chandra Sen in Maharashtra. MG Ranade joined the samaj in 1870. Other leaders were RG Bhandarkar and NG Chandavarkar. Motilal Nehru was not associated with Prarthana Sabha.

subject: Modern History
question_text: The Landholders Society was founded to look after the interests of which class? [SSC CPO 04/10/2023]
solution:
(D) Landlords
The Landholders Society (Zamindari Association) was founded by Dwarakanath Tagore in 1838 to protect interests of landlords, particularly in Bengal. It was launched in Calcutta and was the first political association of Modern India. In 1851, it was merged with Bengal British India society and formed British India Association.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Lala Lajpat Rai was an active member of which organisation? [SSC MTS 16/05/2023]
solution:
(A) Arya Samaj
Lala Lajpat Rai was the active member of Arya Samaj. He was influenced by Swami Dayanand Saraswati. He joined Arya Samaj during his college years and became one of its prominent leaders. Lala Lajpat Rai was also known as Punjab Kesari.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which of the following movements was founded in Bengal? [SSC MTS 01/09/2023]
solution:
(A) Ramakrishna Mission
Ramakrishna Mission was founded by Swami Vivekananda in 1897. Headquarter of this mission was at Belur Math, Howrah (West Bengal). It was aimed to spread the teachings of Ramkrishna Paramhansa, the guru of Vivekananda and a priest of Kali Mandir, Calcutta. The original name of Ramkrishna Paramhansa was 'Gadodhar Chattopadhyay' and according to him 'Service of man is service of God'.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following founded the Widow Remarriage Association? [SSC MTS 11/05/2023]
solution:
(B) Pandit Vishnu Shastri
Widow Remarriage Association was founded by Vishnu Shastri Pandit in 1853, with name Vidhva Vivaha Uttejaka Mandala. Later, M. G. Ranade formed Widow Remarriage Association in 1861. Karsandas Mulji started patrika 'Satya Prakash' in 1852 in Gujarati, to advocate for widow remarriage.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who had founded the first Women's University in India? [SSC MTS 05/09/2023]
solution:
(A) Dhondo Keshav Karve
The first Women's University in India was founded by Dhondo Keshav Karve, also known as Maharshi Karve. It was established in 1916 as the 'Indian Women's University', later renamed as Shreemati Nathibai Damodar Thackersey (SNDT) Women's University. Dhondo Keshav Karve was awarded Bharat Ratna in 1958.

subject: Modern History
question_text: In which language was the work of Raja Ram Mohan Roy, 'Gift to Monotheists' originally written? [SSC CHSL 03/08/2023]
solution:
(B) Persian
The work of Raja Ram Mohan Roy, 'Gift to Monotheists' (Tuhfat-ul-Muwahhidin) was originally written in Persian with Introduction in Arabic. It discusses the superiority of monotheism over polytheism.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Narayan Guru, a social reformer from Ezhava caste and who propagated an anti-caste movement, was born in which of the following states of India? [SSC CHSL 03/08/2023]
solution:
(B) Kerala
Narayan Guru was from Kerala.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who was the first social reformer to view modern education as a vehicle for the spread of modern ideas in the country? [SSC CGL 17/07/2023]
solution:
(A) Raja Ram Mohan Roy
Raja Ram Mohan Roy was the first social reformer to view modern education as vehicle for the spread of modern ideas in the country. He founded Hindu College in 1817 in Calcutta with the help of David Hare. He established Vedanta College in 1825.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following was the founder of the Aligarh Movement, which was largely responsible for the revival of Muslims in India? [SSC CGL 18/07/2023]
solution:
(D) Sayyid Ahmad Khan
Aligarh movement was started by Syed Ahmad Khan in 1875, which was responsible for revival of Muslims in India. It was aimed at modernizing Muslim society and education. He started 'Tahzeeb-ul-Akhlaq', a monthly journal in 1870. Syed Ahmad Khan is often criticized as the 'Father of two-nation theory'.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Ramakrishna Gopal Bhandarkar and Mahadev Govind Ranade carried out their work of religious reforms in Maharashtra through which of the following organisations? [SSC CGL 19/07/2023]
solution:
(A) Prarthana Samaj
Prarthana Samaj (1867) by Atmaram Pandurang in Maharashtra. Atmiya Sabha (1814) and Brahmo Samaj (1828) by Raja Ram Mohan Roy, Theosophical society (1875) by HP Blavatsky and HS Olcott.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who founded the Tattvabodhini Sabha that was set up to explore spiritual truth? [SSC CGL 26/07/2023]
solution:
(C) Debendranath Tagore
Tattvabodhini Sabha, 'Truth-seeking Society' was a reformist group established in 1839 by Debendranath Tagore in Calcutta to promote Hinduism based on Vedanta and Upanishads. Published Bengali Journal 'Tattvabodhini Patrika'. Debendranath Tagore also wrote Brahmo Covenant.

subject: Modern History
question_text: The Veda Samaj of Madras was inspired by the ________. [SSC CGL 27/06/2023]
solution:
(A) Brahmo Samaj
Veda Samaj of Madras was founded in 1864 by Keshab Chandra Sen and K. Sridharalu Naidu. It was considered as the Brahmo Samaj of South India. Keshab Chandra Sen established the Brahmo Samaj of India in 1866 which led to the formation of Sadharan Brahmo Samaj in 1878.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Some of Swami Dayanand's followers started a network of schools and colleges called D.A.V. What does 'A' stand for in D.A.V.? [SSC MTS 28/06/2023]
solution:
(C) Anglo
Dayanand Anglo Vedic (DAV) schools and colleges were founded by Arya Samaj, to promote education on the values of Swami Dayanand Saraswati. The first DAV school was established in 1886 by Mahatma Hansraj in Lahore.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Who among the following founded the Dharma Sabha? [CAPF 2021]
solution:
(C) Radhakant Deb
Radhakanta Deb was a leader of conservative Hindu Society in Calcutta. He established Dharma Sabha in 1830 in Calcutta. It was a revivalist movement and was against the Brahmo Samaj. Radhakanta Deb supported women's education but opposed Sati ban and widow remarriage act of 1856.

subject: Modern History
question_text: The Self-Respect Movement was initiated by: [CDS II 2021]
solution:
(C) E.V. Ramaswamy Naicker
The Self Respect Movement was started by EV Ramaswamy Naicker also known as 'Periyar' in 1924 in Tamil Nadu. It was aimed to challenge caste discrimination and promote social equality. Periyar is considered as the founder of Dravidian movement in Tamil Nadu. Narayan Guru in Kerala and Jyotiba Phule in Maharashtra worked against caste discrimination.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Swami Dayanand Saraswati took inspiration from: [CDS II 2021]
solution:
(B) Vedas
Swami Dayanand Saraswati took inspiration from the Vedas and considered them to be 'India's Rock of Ages', the infallible and the true original seed of Hinduism. He gave the slogan 'Back to Vedas' and worked for the revival of Vedic culture. He criticised Puranas and believed in Karma.

subject: Modern History
question_text: Which of the following ideas was preached by the Kherwar or Sapha Har movement of the 1870s? [CDS II 2021]

options.a: Acceptance of the Hindu pantheonic order

solution:
(B) Monotheism and internal social reform
The Kherwar or Sapha Har movement emerged in 1870s and preached the idea of monotheism (single god) and focused on internal social reforms. It was a tribal movement among the Santhals led by Bhagirath Manjhi.

subject: Modern History
question_text: In which one of the following years was the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha established? [CDS II 2021]
solution:
(D) 1870
Poona Sarvajanik Sabha was founded in 1870 in Pune by Mahadev Govind Ranade with his associates Ganesh Vasudev Joshi, SH Sathe, SH Chiplonkar. It was established as an elected body with 95 members chosen by 6000 people. It served as a precursor to Indian National Congress.

subject: Modern History
question_text:
Consider the following statements about the condition of widows in the 18th century India: [CDS I 2024]
1. Raja Sawai Jai Singh of Amber promoted widow remarriage.
2. Maratha General Parshuram Bhau promoted widow remarriage.
3. Raja Ram Mohan Roy opposed Sati.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
solution:
(D) 1, 2 and 3
